Page 2 of 85 A. Performance and Outlook
Mr Speaker, Sir
1. I beg to move that Parliament approve the financial policy of the
Government for the financial year 1 April 2023 to 31 March 2024.
Looking Back at 2022
2. Sir, we have been fighting COVID-19 for more than three years.
a. It has been a long, hard slog, full of unexpected twists and
turns, and I thank everyone for your cooperation, hard work,
and sacrifices.
b. Through our collective efforts, we have found our way
through this pandemic.

orts, we have found our way
through this pandemic. c. Yesterday we reached a new milestone and moved to
DORSCON green.
3. In parallel, industries that were hardest hit by COVID-19 are
seeing continued recovery.
a. Demand has picked up for sectors like F&B and retail.
Page 3 of 85

d up for sectors like F&B and retail.
Page 3 of 85 b. Construction activities are back in full swing.
c. Air travel has resumed, and Singaporeans are travelling again.
SQ flights are filling up, pilots and cabin crew are busy flying,
and Changi Airport is buzzing with life.
4. All in all, amidst a very challenging external backdrop, our
economy grew by 3.6% in 2022. The resident unemployment rate, at
2.8% in December last year, is below pre-pandemic levels.
5. At the start of 2022, we had anticipated tightness in energy

rt of 2022, we had anticipated tightness in energy markets and supply chain bottlenecks.
a. But the outbreak of the Ukraine war caught many by surprise.
b. Despite it happening halfway across the world, the war set
off a chain reaction of shockwaves throughout the global
economy.
c. Oil and gas prices shot up; we experienced a global shortage
of grain and other food items.
d. Some countries also imposed export bans to secure their own
domestic needs, worsening global supply bottlenecks.
Page 4 of 85

worsening global supply bottlenecks.
Page 4 of 85 e. Inflation was further fuelled by tight labour markets across
many countries, partly because those who left the workforce
when COVID-19 hit have not all returned. And companies had
to pay higher wages to attract workers, adding to inflationary
momentum.
6. So as many have observed, 2022 was a year of brutal inflation
worldwide.1
a. By the end of last year, global inflation was around 9%.2
b. Inflation reached historic levels in many advanced economies

reached historic levels in many advanced economies – in the US, consumer prices were the highest in four decades;
in Germany, they were the highest in nearly half a century.
7. Singapore, too, had to contend with these inflationary pressures.
a. MAS tightened our monetary policy five times since
October 2021.
1 “2022 has been a year of brutal inflation”, The Economist, 21 December 2022
2 Source: IMF World Economic Outlook
Page 5 of 85

2 Source: IMF World Economic Outlook
Page 5 of 85 b. While this has helped ease the pressure on headline inflation,
it is still higher than what Singaporeans are used to.
c. So to address cost-of-living concerns, I rolled out three
packages last year, totalling more than $3.5 billion, extending
comprehensive support to Singaporeans, especially the
lower- and middle-income families.
FY2022 Fiscal Position
8. We were able to fund these support packages from stronger-
than-expected revenues, especially from corporate income tax and

revenues, especially from corporate income tax and asset-related taxes in FY2022.
9. This additional revenue will also help to cover higher spending in
other areas.
a. In particular, HDB has ramped up its BTO supply to catch up
with the backlog that had arisen from the COVID-19 delays.
b. This means building more flats at a time when construction
and land costs have increased, which in turn requires more
funding resources.
Page 6 of 85

turn requires more
funding resources.
Page 6 of 85 10. In FY2022, we also drew on Past Reserves to fund our emergency
COVID-19 public health expenditures.
a. The President had earlier concurred with a draw of up to
$6 billion from Past Reserves specifically for this purpose.
b. Because the public health situation has since stabilised, we
now expect to draw a lower amount of up to $3.1 billion from
Past Reserves.
11. This brings the total expected draw on Past Reserves from FY2020
to FY2022 to $40 billion. (See Annex A-1.)

FY2020
to FY2022 to $40 billion. (See Annex A-1.) a. This is lower than the initial draw of $52 billion that the
Government had sought the President’s agreement for.
b. It reflects our prudent approach in using our reserves –
drawing on them judiciously, only when there are compelling
reasons to do so.
12. So all in all, putting together our revenue upside with our higher
spending, we expect a slight deficit of $2 billion, or 0.3% of GDP, for
FY2022. (See Annex A-2.)
Page 7 of 85

of GDP, for
FY2022. (See Annex A-2.)
Page 7 of 85 a. We last drew on Past Reserves during the Global Financial
Crisis in 2008. We spent $4 billion in FY2009 and were able to
put back what we drew two years later due to the sharp
recovery in the economy and in our fiscal position.
b. This time, we are in a different position.
c. Our economy has recovered back to pre-COVID levels. But we
continue to be in a tight fiscal position.
d. It is therefore highly unlikely that we will be able to put back
what we have drawn from Past Reserves.

to put back
what we have drawn from Past Reserves. 13. But we will not waver from our commitment to safeguard our
reserves as a key strategic asset. They have helped Singapore to
weather major global shocks, prevent high unemployment rates, and
build up our capabilities even in the midst of economic downturns.
Other governments spent more during this pandemic too. But they
largely financed their additional spending by borrowing, which will
eventually have to be repaid by future generations. In contrast, our

be repaid by future generations. In contrast, our reserves allowed Singapore to respond quickly without falling into
debt, or burdening either current or future generations of
Singaporeans.
Page 8 of 85

r future generations of
Singaporeans.
Page 8 of 85 14. So we will continue to uphold our practice of fiscal prudence and
the principles that underpin the protection of our reserves.
a. And this is why it was necessary to raise the GST rate – to
ensure we have the resources to take good care of our
seniors, and to keep a balanced budget over the medium
term.
b. I thank Singaporeans for your understanding of why we must
continue to live within our means and contribute our fair
share of revenues, and be good stewards of our reserves for

revenues, and be good stewards of our reserves for the benefit of all Singaporeans – both now and in the future.
Outlook for 2023
15. For 2023, the key factor that will shape our growth is the global
economy.
a. For now, the picture is mixed and uneven, because while
there are weaknesses in many parts of the world, the IMF and
other economists do not expect a global recession this year.
b. The EU is amongst the hardest-hit due to the economic
consequences of its exposure to the war in Ukraine.
Page 9 of 85

f its exposure to the war in Ukraine.
Page 9 of 85 c. The US is seeing slower growth, and may enter a recession,
as the Federal Reserve continues to raise interest rates to
combat elevated inflation. Even so, many economists expect
any contraction to be mild.
d. Asia is expected to continue growing this year. In particular,
the COVID-19 infection wave in China has probably peaked.
As China’s COVID-19 situation stabilises, and it continues to
re-open its economy, the pickup in demand should provide a
boost to the global economy.

mand should provide a
boost to the global economy. 16. Overall, based on current indications of global conditions, we
expect positive but slower economic growth in Singapore of 0.5% to
2.5% this year.
17. But our economic fundamentals remain strong. Our handling of
the pandemic over the past three years has enhanced our reputation
as a reliable and trusted node in global supply chains. Our deep
relationships with both the US and China, as well as partners in ASEAN
and the wider region, make us a neutral and increasingly important

gion, make us a neutral and increasingly important place for global and regional businesses. We will take full advantage
of these opportunities to attract new flows of investments, capital,
talent, and ideas. All these will add vibrancy to our economy and
create more good jobs for Singaporeans.
Page 10 of 85

ate more good jobs for Singaporeans.
Page 10 of 85 18. But there are major uncertainties and downside risks to this
year’s forecast.
a. A greater-than-expected decline in the US and EU economies
could tip the world into recession.
b. We may see an escalation of the Ukraine war or a prolonged
conflict and stalemate, leading to new waves of disruptions
that further weigh on global trade, as well as consumer and
business confidence.
c. We also cannot rule out a new COVID-19 variant that could
be more dangerous and virulent.

variant that could
be more dangerous and virulent. 19. Whether or when these events will happen cannot be predicted
with certainty.
a. But we must prepare ourselves for these risks and be ready to
respond to them.
b. We will therefore develop drawer plans, so that we have the
ability and resources to take swift action should these
downside scenarios materialise.
Page 11 of 85

hese
downside scenarios materialise.
Page 11 of 85 B. Dealing with Inflation
20. Inflation is the other major uncertainty in the global economy
and a major concern for the world, and for Singapore.
21. And there are some early signs that global headline inflation rates
are softening. But it is premature to declare victory.
a. China’s re-opening will mean more demand for commodities,
especially oil and gas, pushing up prices. And this will come
at a time when Europe is still trying to secure more energy
supplies.

pe is still trying to secure more energy
supplies. b. There are also signs of more persistent underlying
inflationary pressures, especially with tight labour markets
around the world.
22. We therefore have to brace ourselves for a period of relatively
higher inflation, both globally and also in Singapore. We cannot say
how long this will last, but we expect Singapore’s headline inflation to
remain high, at least for the first half of this year.
23. The Government will therefore do more to help Singaporeans
through this difficult period.

o help Singaporeans
through this difficult period. Page 12 of 85

reans
through this difficult period. Page 12 of 85 24. We will help businesses weather the immediate challenges of
tighter financial conditions and higher energy prices.
25. I will extend the current enhancements to the Enterprise
Financing Scheme for another year till 31 March 2024. This includes
the 70% Government risk-share for trade loans, the enhanced
maximum quantum for trade and working capital loans, and support
for domestic construction projects via project loans. I hope this will

ction projects via project loans. I hope this will also encourage financial institutions to continue extending credit to
viable enterprises.
26. I will also extend the Energy Efficiency Grant for one year until
31 March 2024. This will provide continued support for businesses in
the Food Services, Food Manufacturing, and Retail sectors to invest in
energy efficiency, and thereby reduce the impact of higher electricity
prices.
Enhancement of Support Measures for Singaporeans
27. We will also help Singaporeans tide through this period of higher

lp Singaporeans tide through this period of higher inflation, and cushion the impact of the new GST rates.
28. There are two prongs to the support we provide for Singaporeans.
Page 13 of 85

support we provide for Singaporeans.
Page 13 of 85 a. One is the permanent GST Voucher, or GSTV scheme, which
helps to permanently defray GST expenses for lower- to
middle-income Singaporeans.
b. The second is the Assurance Package, which provides
transitional support to all Singaporeans to cushion the impact
of the higher GST rate.
29. In last year’s Budget, I enhanced the permanent GSTV scheme
and increased the number of Singaporeans who benefit from it. I will
further enhance the permanent GSTV scheme so that it continues to

the permanent GSTV scheme so that it continues to meaningfully defray GST expenses for eligible households.
a. For those residing in homes with Annual Values of $13,000
and below, I will increase the GSTV Cash quantum from $500
to $700 in 2023, and to a further $850 from 2024 onwards.
b. For those residing in homes with Annual Values of above
$13,000 and up to $21,000, I will increase the GSTV Cash
quantum from $250 to $350 in 2023, and then to $450 from
2024 onwards.
30. These enhancements to the permanent GSTV scheme ensure

e enhancements to the permanent GSTV scheme ensure that most retiree as well as lower-income households will not be
Page 14 of 85

lower-income households will not be
Page 14 of 85 impacted by the GST rate increase. As I have emphasised previously,
under our GST system – which means the GST and the GSTV combined
– the more well-off consumers, as well as foreigners and tourists, will
bear higher effective GST rates than lower-income Singaporeans. This
ensures that those with greater means contribute their fair share of
taxes, and will effectively lower the burden of taxes on lower- and
middle-income Singaporeans.

of taxes on lower- and
middle-income Singaporeans. 31. On top of the GSTV, we have the Assurance Package, or the AP,
to cushion the impact of the GST rate increase on Singaporeans. In
November last year, I announced that I would review and update the
package to account for higher inflation. We have completed the
review and will make the following enhancements in this Budget:
a. I will increase the AP Cash by between $300 and $650 for
eligible Singaporeans over the remaining years of the
Assurance Package. This will bring the total AP Cash

surance Package. This will bring the total AP Cash payments received by adult Singaporeans to between $700
and $2,250 over five years.
b. I will increase the CDC Vouchers by $100 in 2024. So all
Singaporean households can look forward to another $300 of
CDC vouchers in January next year.
Page 15 of 85

f
CDC vouchers in January next year.
Page 15 of 85 32. These enhancements to the Assurance Package will enable us to
maintain the commitments we had set out earlier, which is to offset
additional GST expenses for at least five years for the majority of
Singaporean households, and for about 10 years for the lower-income
households.
33. But I recognise that many Singaporeans are still concerned about
the immediate cost-of-living issues. I will therefore introduce
additional one-off support measures this year under the Assurance
Package.

rt measures this year under the Assurance
Package. a. I will provide a Cost-of-Living Special Payment of between
$200 and $400 for each eligible adult Singaporean.
b. I will provide additional support for seniors and extend a
Cost-of-Living Seniors’ Bonus of between $200 and $300 for
eligible Singaporeans aged 55 and above.
c. I will also double the U-Save Rebates provided to households
over the next three tranches of disbursement this year. In
total, eligible households can expect to receive up to $760 in
U-Save Rebates this year.
Page 16 of 85

to $760 in
U-Save Rebates this year.
Page 16 of 85 d. For households with children, I will provide each child aged
six and below a top-up of $400 to their Child Development
Account, and each older child a top-up of $300 to their
Edusave account or Post-Secondary Education Account.
34. These enhancements to the Assurance Package, both the one-off
and earlier ones, will cost $3 billion. So the total amount of the
Package will increase from $6.6 billion to $9.6 billion.
35. With the enhancements to the GSTV scheme and Assurance

the enhancements to the GSTV scheme and Assurance Package, I will have provided comprehensive help for the majority of
Singaporean households this year. On average,
a. For lower-income households, the enhancements will fully
cover the increases in spending due to inflation and the GST
rate increase.
b. For middle-income households, the enhancements will
substantially cover their increases in spending.
36. Let me illustrate what Singaporeans can expect from these
measures:
Page 17 of 85

eans can expect from these
measures:
Page 17 of 85 a. A lower-income family with two young children will receive
about $5,500 in benefits. This includes cash, Government
top-ups to the CDA for their children, MediSave top-ups,
U-Save and S&CC Rebates, and CDC Vouchers.
b. Help will also be extended to middle-income households.
c. And we have ensured that larger households, particularly
those with seniors and children staying together, will receive
more support. For example, a six-person middle-income

e support. For example, a six-person middle-income household with two seniors and two school-going children
will receive about $8,400 in benefits.
37. The full details of the enhancements to the GSTV scheme and
Assurance Package are in the Annex. (See Annex B-1.)
38. The updates to the Assurance Package reflect the commitment
of this Government to help Singaporeans through this challenging
period of higher prices. We provided comprehensive support last year,
and we are doing so again this year.

rt last year,
and we are doing so again this year. 39. But I hope all Singaporeans understand that it is not fiscally
sustainable to rely so heavily on Government support year after year
to cope with inflation.
Page 18 of 85

r after year
to cope with inflation.
Page 18 of 85 40. The reality is that even after the current inflation surge
moderates, inflation may stabilise at a higher trend level globally, and
in Singapore, than what we were used to in the last few decades. The
era of untrammelled globalisation that kept goods at highly
competitive prices all over the world is over. Countries are now
relooking and adjusting their supply chains. Instead of buying from the
cheapest, they are prepared to accept lower efficiency and higher

are prepared to accept lower efficiency and higher costs to prioritise diversification and strategic resilience. These trends
are pushing up inflation everywhere, including Singapore.
41. We do not have much influence over this global inflation picture.
But our best strategy to cope with inflation is to make ourselves more
productive and competitive – so that our workers earn more, and the
increase in earnings can more than make up for the higher prices.
Indeed, this was what happened last year – real incomes grew by 2.0%

hat happened last year – real incomes grew by 2.0% for the median worker, and by a higher 4.7% for a worker in the
20th percentile.3 So despite higher prices, we are still better off in real
terms.
3 Based on latest figures in the Ministry of Manpower’s Labour Force in Singapore 2022 report.
Page 19 of 85

bour Force in Singapore 2022 report.
Page 19 of 85 42. We must therefore press on with economic restructuring and
transformation, help businesses raise their productivity, and help
workers upgrade their skills at every opportunity.
Page 20 of 85

e their skills at every opportunity.
Page 20 of 85 C. Moving Forward in a New Era
43. Sir, this Budget is about building our capabilities and seizing new
opportunities in a new era of global development.
44. The geopolitical context has shifted significantly. Great power
contestation has intensified, most notably between the US and China.
And this has profound implications for the world.
45. For the last three decades since the end of the Cold War, the
world has benefited from global economic integration. Countries

efited from global economic integration. Countries everywhere embraced openness in global commerce. Multinational
Enterprises, or MNEs, developed their supply chains based on the
most cost-efficient configurations around the world.
a. But now, businesses are re-organising themselves to account
for once-unimaginable tail risks, including geopolitical risks.
b. More and more MNEs are looking to re-shore, on-shore, or
near-shore – that is, relocating factories and offices to places
where they are less likely to get caught up in geo-strategic

are less likely to get caught up in geo-strategic crossfires.
Page 21 of 85

ught up in geo-strategic crossfires.
Page 21 of 85 46. Governments in the developed world are also embracing more
muscular industrial policies. Despite their macro-economic policies
being generally accommodative over the past decade, growth in many
of these advanced economies has been sluggish. Incomes of middle-
earners have stagnated and unemployment rates are high. They are
therefore looking for ways to rebuild their manufacturing capabilities
and create jobs for their people.
47. The US in particular is determined to establish a clear

S in particular is determined to establish a clear technological lead over China, especially in critical industries like semi-
conductors and green energy. It has banned the flow of high-end chips
and chipmaking to China, and is engaging its allies around the world
to do the same. China in turn is investing in its own “little giants” to
achieve technological self-reliance. We are seeing a huge contest for
leadership in key technologies, which is likely to escalate with time.
48. All this is setting off a dangerous dynamic towards greater

is setting off a dangerous dynamic towards greater economic nationalism and protectionism around the world.
a. Governments are stumping out more aggressive support, in
the form of tax breaks and subsidies, to anchor “strategic”
industries.
Page 22 of 85

s, to anchor “strategic”
industries.
Page 22 of 85 b. The major powers increasingly view economic matters
through the lens of national security. Sanctions, investment
prohibitions, and export controls are just some of the tools
that they are using to safeguard national security.
49. In short, we must expect to see greater contestation and
fragmentation in the global economy. Countries are thinking less
about mutual benefit and interdependence, and more about national
gain and security. An era of zero-sum thinking has begun. It will be a

n era of zero-sum thinking has begun. It will be a world that is less hospitable to small economies like Singapore, which
have long thrived on an open, rules-based multilateral system.
50. We therefore cannot assume that we can continue to be
successful by doing the same things as we have in the past. We will
need to adjust to this new era, reposition our economy, and refresh
our social compact for the future.
51. This is not the first time that we have had to adapt to changes in
the world. And it will certainly not be the last.

the world. And it will certainly not be the last. a. We have done it many times before, through many crises.
b. And each time, we emerged with a stronger economy and a
more united people.
Page 23 of 85

er economy and a
more united people.
Page 23 of 85 c. And I am confident we will do it again.
52. The key to our success is the trust we have in one another, and
our solidarity and unity as a nation. This year marks the 100th
anniversary of the birth of Mr Lee Kuan Yew. I remember reading what
he had said in 1967, soon after the turbulent period of Singapore’s
independence. He said, and I quote: “There is tranquillity, poise and
confidence in Singapore. And it is a confidence born out of the

Singapore. And it is a confidence born out of the knowledge that there are very few problems which we cannot
overcome… [for] our people’s natural industry and talent will continue
to blossom and flourish and generate prosperity for all.”
53. Indeed, there are very few problems we cannot overcome when
we apply ourselves as one people. It is up to us to overcome the
problems we face, to improve on what we have today, and to build
the Singapore we want for tomorrow. This is why I am glad that many

want for tomorrow. This is why I am glad that many Singaporeans have stepped forward to join us in the Forward
Singapore exercise, sharing with us their aspirations and concerns,
and joining hands and hearts to create our new social compact
together.
54. Our engagements so far have affirmed the shared purpose and
values that underpin our social compact. We aspire to be a fairer and
Page 24 of 85

ompact. We aspire to be a fairer and
Page 24 of 85 more inclusive society, and a kinder and more gracious Singapore –
one where we value one another for who we are, share the
responsibility of supporting each other, and uphold our sense of
identity in a harmonious, multi-racial, and multi-religious nation.
55. To achieve these shared aspirations, the Government is pursuing
new strategies in a few key areas:
a. How we can continue to uplift the wages of those at the lower
end of the income distribution, and sustain real income

f the income distribution, and sustain real income growth for workers in the broad middle.
b. How we can support workers, especially those who are
displaced, with a better system of reskilling and upskilling, so
that they can be re-trained and placed into new jobs, and
bounce back from setbacks in life.
c. How we can give everyone opportunities throughout their
lives to uplift themselves, so that the circumstances of birth
will not determine how far they go, and we can keep social
mobility alive and well in Singapore.

keep social
mobility alive and well in Singapore. d. And how we can better look after our growing number of
seniors – not just their medical and retirement needs, but
Page 25 of 85

ir medical and retirement needs, but
Page 25 of 85 also their care and living arrangements, so that they can age
in place with grace, dignity, and security.
56. These are important but complex issues which require further
exploration. We aim to complete the Forward Singapore exercise in
the second half of this year. It is not just a matter of having the
Government do more to provide greater assurance and support. We
will make changes and intervene boldly where there is a need to do
so. But government actions must reinforce the values of personal

ment actions must reinforce the values of personal effort, responsibility for the family, and mutual support in the
community. These are values that keep our society strong and enable
our people to stand with pride and contribute to society. At the same
time, we must ensure that any additional spending can be funded and
sustained well into the future, so that we do what is right not just for
today, but also for future generations.
57. That said, there are several pressing issues that have emerged in

e are several pressing issues that have emerged in our conversations, which we will address in this Budget. We know
that long wait times for new flats and rising resale home prices are
key concerns for many young Singaporeans. We had a long discussion
and debate on this in Parliament. We also want to help parents better
balance their work and family commitments, and look after their
children. So, we will move sooner on specific measures to support
Page 26 of 85

oner on specific measures to support
Page 26 of 85 young married couples, and create a more conducive environment
for families.
58. The Budget this year is therefore part of a broader exercise to
chart our new way forward together, and it is centred on three key
thrusts to secure our future in a new era.
a. We will grow our economy, equip our workers, and provide a
fuller range of opportunities for everyone to advance in life.
b. We will strengthen our social compact and provide better
support for families, seniors, and vulnerable groups.

port for families, seniors, and vulnerable groups. c. And we will build up our collective resilience so that we are
able to bounce back strengthened from external shocks and
setbacks.
59. Let me go through each of these thrusts.
Page 27 of 85

me go through each of these thrusts.
Page 27 of 85 D. Growing Our Economy and Equipping Our Workers
60. Economic competition will get stiffer in this new era where
countries compete for power and influence. Singapore will have to
adapt quickly to these changes, to survive and prosper in a troubled
world.
Building Capabilities and Anchoring Quality Investments
61. We will therefore redouble our efforts to attract high-quality
investments. We will focus on growth sectors where we can be highly

ill focus on growth sectors where we can be highly competitive. For example, we are a leading international financial
centre in Asia. We are a global trading hub with strengths in
transportation and logistics, anchored by our air and sea ports. We
also have a vibrant manufacturing sector, supported by key segments
like electronics, chemicals, and biomedical science.
62. These attributes and strengths will enable us to attract more
MNEs to anchor their regional, or even their global operations and

heir regional, or even their global operations and headquarters in Singapore. With more high-value activities and best-
in-class facilities based here, we will be able to build new capabilities,
develop key industries, and create good jobs for Singaporeans. We
will also create spin-offs for our local enterprises as they serve the
MNEs based here, plug into global networks, and eventually grow to
become industry leaders in their own right.
Page 28 of 85

industry leaders in their own right.
Page 28 of 85 63. But we must expect global competition for investments to get
tougher. With the Base Erosion and Profit Shifting initiative, or
BEPS 2.0, on the horizon, we will have less scope to use tax incentives
to attract new investments. Meanwhile, the US and other countries
are rolling out vast subsidies to build up their strategic industries. It
will not be possible for us to outbid these countries with even bigger
subsidies just to get their MNEs to invest here. But neither can we

get their MNEs to invest here. But neither can we afford to be complacent, and simply take our competitive position for
granted.
64. We will therefore have to work harder to enhance our overall
productivity and workforce quality to stay competitive in this new
environment. We currently have the National Productivity Fund,
which supports a wide range of measures for productivity
enhancement, and continuing education and training.
65. I will top up the National Productivity Fund with $4 billion, and

he National Productivity Fund with $4 billion, and expand the scope of the Fund to include investment promotion as a
supportable activity. We will use the fund to anchor more quality
investments here. This includes supporting companies to build new
capabilities, add greater value to our domestic ecosystems, and
upskill our workers. And ultimately, these efforts will lead to better-
paying jobs for Singaporeans.
Page 29 of 85

etter-
paying jobs for Singaporeans.
Page 29 of 85 Nurturing and Sustaining Innovation
66. Besides anchoring investments and deepening capabilities, we
must nurture and sustain pervasive innovation across the economy.
We have to push new frontiers, and differentiate ourselves in quality
and value.
67. This is why the Government has consistently committed
resources to R&D.
a. We are investing $25 billion from 2021 to 2025 to catalyse
research, innovation, and enterprise.
b. We are restructuring and raising our game, industry by

re restructuring and raising our game, industry by industry, through the Industry Transformation Maps.
68. Our efforts are bearing fruit. But innovation is not without risk,
which businesses may find difficult to undertake amidst slower
growth and higher cost.
69. So to help our businesses press on with innovation, I will
introduce a new Enterprise Innovation Scheme.
Page 30 of 85

ure
Singapore and aligned to the Skills Framework. 70. Today, businesses can enjoy tax deductions of up to 250% of
qualifying expenditure on some of these activities. I will raise the tax
deductions to 400% of qualifying expenditure on each of these five
activities. The qualifying expenditure will be capped at $400,000 for
each activity, except for innovation carried out with Polytechnics and
ITE, for which the expenditure will be capped at $50,000. With these
enhancements, businesses that make full use of the scheme could

businesses that make full use of the scheme could enjoy tax savings of nearly 70% of their investment.
71. Some firms have yet to turn profitable, or do not have sufficient
profits to maximise the benefits from the tax deductions. To support
these firms, I will allow businesses the option to convert 20% of their
total qualifying expenditure per Year of Assessment into a cash

Page 2 of 82 A. Introduction
Mr Speaker,
1. I move that Parliament approves the financial policy of the
Government for the Financial Year 1 April 2026 to 31 March 2027.
2. This is our first Budget in this new term of Government. It
comes as we enter a post-SG60 phase in our nation building journey.
3. We begin this next phase at a time of profound global change.

an you use your OA savings for when buying a home? Your OA savings can be used to buy a HDB flat, or buy or build private and residential properties in Singapore. CPF savings are for your future retirement needs! Use a mix of cash and your OA savings for your housing payments, so that you can keep some monies in your OA to earn attractive interest rates. Your OA savings can also act as a safety net for your housing payments.

Features
Amount to refund when selling your property

atures
Amount to refund when selling your property CPF is meant for your retirement needs. When you used your CPF savings for your property, your retirement savings is reduced. Hence, when you sell your property, you will need to refund the amount that you have used plus the interest accrued on this amount. This is to restore your retirement savings.
If you have pledged the property to make up your retirement sum, you will also need to refund the pledged amount.

the conjunctural developments and outlook for the global and
domestic economies. These are integral components of MAS’
monetary policy decisions.
In this issue of the Review, EPG is also pleased to present Special
Feature B, which examines the effectiveness of public investment as a
countercyclical policy tool from theoretical and empirical perspectives.

Steven J. Davis, the Thomas W. and Susan B. Ford Senior Fellow at the
Hoover Institution, Stanford University, and Advisor, EPG, for
contributing Special Feature C, which analyses the effects of work from
home arrangements on wages and productivity in the US.

Monetary Policy Statement
INTRODUCTION
1. In the January 2024 Monetary Policy Statement (MPS), MAS maintained the rate of
appreciation of the Singapore dollar nominal effective exchange rate (S$NEER) policy band, with no
change to the width of the band or level at which it was centred. Since then, the S$NEER has
continued to strengthen in the upper half of the appreciating policy band.
Chart 1

pper half of the appreciating policy band.
Chart 1 S$ Nominal Effective Exchange Rate (S$NEER)
GROWTH BACKDROP
2. Global economic growth remained resilient at the turn of the year. In the near term, growth in
Singapore’s major trading partners is expected to be tempered by the impact of past monetary policy
tightening and withdrawal of previous expansionary fiscal policies. Towards the later part of 2024,
final demand should pick up in line with the anticipated easing of global monetary policy. Global

ticipated easing of global monetary policy. Global manufacturing should also remain on its recovery path. This outlook is subject to uncertainties,
including around the pace and timing of monetary policy easing and the intensity of ongoing
geopolitical conflicts.
3. MTI’s Advance Estimates show that growth in the Singapore economy came in at 0.1% on a
quarter-on-quarter seasonally-adjusted basis in the first quarter of 2024, down from 1.2% in Q4 2023.
Manufacturing and modern services activity saw some slowing in Q1 2024 after having expanded

saw some slowing in Q1 2024 after having expanded strongly in the preceding quarters. Growth in the consumer-facing sectors picked up in Q1, reflecting
in part the boost from an increase in tourist arrivals.

rt the boost from an increase in tourist arrivals. Monetary Policy Statement 7
4. Prospects for the Singapore economy should improve over the course of 2024, with GDP
growth forecast to come in between 1–3%. The recovery in the manufacturing and financial sectors
should resume, supported by the upturn in the electronics cycle and anticipated easing in global
interest rates. Meanwhile, growth in the domestic-oriented sectors is projected to normalise, slowing
towards pre-pandemic rates.
INFLATION OUTLOOK

wing
towards pre-pandemic rates.
INFLATION OUTLOOK 5. MAS Core Inflation1 averaged 3.4% y-o-y in Jan–Feb. The increase from 3.3% in Q4 2023 was
lower than expected. Inflation edged up due to the step-up in the GST rate, higher electricity & gas
tariffs from the carbon tax hike, as well as increases in essential services fees amid higher input and
labour costs. However, inflation for food and travel-related services slowed and was more modest
than anticipated. Both non-cooked food and food services inflation declined in Jan–Feb, while hotel

ervices inflation declined in Jan–Feb, while hotel room rates abroad also fell sharply after surging late last year. Excluding the impact of the GST
increases, underlying inflation is estimated to have been unchanged in Jan–Feb from Q4 last year.
6. Meanwhile, CPI-All Items inflation fell to 3.1% y-o-y over Jan–Feb, from 4.0% in the preceding
quarter, underpinned by the further reduction in private transport and accommodation inflation. The
slower pace of increase in car prices and residential property rents was due to a greater supply of

tial property rents was due to a greater supply of COE quotas and completed housing units, respectively.
7. Core inflation is forecast to stay elevated in the immediate quarters ahead, before stepping
down more discernibly in Q4 2024 and into 2025. In the near term, core inflation will remain around
current levels as water prices rose in April while prices of certain services, such as education and
healthcare, will continue to catch up to higher costs. Nevertheless, as imported and domestic cost

costs. Nevertheless, as imported and domestic cost pressures continue to abate, underlying inflation should moderate further. Although crude oil prices
rose over the last three months, global prices of most food commodities as well as intermediate and
final goods remain subdued. On the domestic front, wage growth has eased and should moderate
this year as labour market tightness dissipates. Productivity is also expected to pick up.
Consequently, unit labour costs will increase at a significantly slower pace compared to the
preceding two years.

y slower pace compared to the
preceding two years. 8. For 2024 as a whole, both MAS Core Inflation and CPI-All Items inflation are projected to come
in at an average of 2.5–3.5%. Excluding the impact of the increases in the GST rate, core and headline
inflation are forecast at 1.5–2.5%.
9. Both upside and downside risks to the inflation outlook remain. Shocks to global food and
energy prices, or stronger-than-expected demand for labour in the domestic economy, could bring

nd for labour in the domestic economy, could bring about additional inflationary pressures. However, an unexpected weakening in the global economy
could induce a faster easing of cost and price pressures.
MONETARY POLICY
10. The Singapore economy is expected to strengthen over 2024, with growth becoming more
broad-based. The slightly negative output gap is projected to narrow further in H2 2024, even as
underlying inflationary pressures gradually dissipate. MAS Core Inflation is likely to remain elevated

e. MAS Core Inflation is likely to remain elevated in the earlier part of the year but should stay on its broadly moderating path and step down in Q4,
before falling further into 2025.

step down in Q4,
before falling further into 2025. 8 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
Macroeconomic Review | April 2020
11. Accordingly, current monetary policy settings remain appropriate. The prevailing rate of
appreciation of the policy band is needed to keep a restraining effect on imported inflation as well
as domestic cost pressures, and is sufficient to ensure medium-term price stability.
12. MAS will therefore maintain the prevailing rate of appreciation of the S$NEER policy band.

ng rate of appreciation of the S$NEER policy band. There will be no change to its width and the level at which it is centred. MAS will closely monitor
global and domestic economic developments, and remain vigilant to risks to inflation and growth.

inflation. The International Economy 9
1 The International Economy
• The continued resilience of the global economy is evident from the latest
high-frequency indicators in early-2024. The benign growth backdrop will
add momentum to the nascent upturn in the global electronics cycle,
supporting the EM Asia economies. Looking ahead, global growth is
expected to remain steady in 2024, even as the impact of past monetary
policy tightening and less supportive fiscal policy continues to exert a

less supportive fiscal policy continues to exert a restraining effect.
• Following significant progress last year, the pace of disinflation in
Singapore’s major trading partners, especially the AEs, has slowed. The
road ahead is likely to be bumpy as the deflationary impulse from lower
commodity and goods prices fade. Further, geopolitical risks or extreme
weather events could result in supply-driven cost hikes. In the AEs, slower
wage and strong productivity growth will be necessary to return inflation
sustainably to their 2% targets.

return inflation
sustainably to their 2% targets. • Against this backdrop, the balance of risks appears to be still tilted
towards persistent inflation. However, a delayed and slower pace of AE
monetary easing could trigger latent financial vulnerabilities and
consequently weigh on growth. For EM Asia, the combination of high AE
bond yields, increased risk aversion, and USD appreciation would
exacerbate the spillovers from weaker global growth and sentiment,
imparting stresses on regional asset prices and those economies with

on regional asset prices and those economies with substantial external financing needs.
1.1 Global Economic Developments
The global economy is still showing notable resilience, despite slower growth
Global economic growth moderated in Q4 2023 as the outperformance of the US was
offset by weaker growth across several ASEAN-5 economies. Still, at 3.3% for 2023 as a
whole, the expansion showed considerable strength in the face of multi-decade high interest

strength in the face of multi-decade high interest rates (Charts 1.1 and 1.2). Recent high-frequency data signal that the resilience has persisted
into 2024, reflecting in part the revival in manufacturing activities. Outperforming economies
continued to turn in strong growth, while tentative signs of recovery were also evident in
several laggard economies.
In the US, growth momentum has remained robust, as employment and wages continued
to expand above pre-pandemic trends, bolstering household spending. Investment in

ends, bolstering household spending. Investment in manufacturing structures has surged, in part due to incentives under the Inflation Reduction
Act (IRA) and CHIPS and Science Act. There is emerging evidence that equipment investment
is picking up as construction of factories reach completion. Meanwhile, investment in
artificial intelligence (AI) continues to grow at a healthy clip. Encouragingly, several business
surveys point to a long-awaited rebalancing between manufacturing and services, with the
ncing between manufacturing and services, with the ISM manufacturing index marking an expansion in March for the first time in over a year.

ansion in March for the first time in over a year. 10 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
Meanwhile, China’s economy experienced renewed momentum in H2 2023 after the
post-pandemic recovery initially lost steam in the earlier months. The pickup has carried over
to 2024, especially in manufacturing, while overall exports has also firmed. Nonetheless, the
property market remains in the doldrums with transaction volumes and prices continuing to
deteriorate. Across the region, the upturn in manufacturing is showing tentative signs of

urn in manufacturing is showing tentative signs of broadening beyond semiconductors as evidenced by the expansion in the ASEAN-5’s latest
manufacturing PMI and new orders index. With the destocking of manufacturing inventories
in 2023 largely completed, regional industrial production has picked up, following lacklustre
growth last year.

1.2 Global Outlook
Global growth is poised for continued steady expansion over 2024
The resilience that the global economy has exhibited so far is set to continue for the rest
of the year. Notably, the US economy is expected to turn in fairly robust growth. Excess
savings built over the pandemic and increases in net wealth have created a virtuous cycle of

ses in net wealth have created a virtuous cycle of strong household spending, improved corporate profitability and solid labour market
conditions (Chart 1.3). Even as excess savings diminish, and real interest rates and fiscal
policy turn more restrictive over 2024, the strength of private sector balance sheets and
stronger real wage growth will continue to support around trend growth (Chart 1.4).
In contrast, economic momentum in the Eurozone and Japan is expected to remain

um in the Eurozone and Japan is expected to remain relatively sluggish in the short term. However, activity should improve in H2 as inflation eases
and real disposable incomes and financial conditions improve.

China’s GDP growth is forecast to ease in 2024, even as the larger projected fiscal deficit

2024, even as the larger projected fiscal deficit this year imparts a positive impulse to the economy. In any event, the government retains

For the rest of EM Asia, growth is set to pick up across the ASEAN-5 and NEA-2 blocs
amid a recovery in manufacturing and goods exports. However, the improvement in ASEAN

d goods exports. However, the improvement in ASEAN goods exports is likely to lag that of the NEA, given the former’s largely downstream position

growth in the ASEAN-5 given solid labour market conditions and public investment plans.
Further, the ongoing recovery in tourism, especially the renewed inflows of Chinese tourists,
will benefit the ASEAN-5. China was the world’s largest consumer of travel services in 2019

orld’s largest consumer of travel services in 2019 in terms of both departure numbers and spending. Regaining some of these pre-pandemic

All in, the global economy is expected to expand by 3.0% in 2024, slightly lower than the
3.3% recorded last year, as the impact of past policy tightening and less supportive fiscal
policy in most economies continues to exert a restraining effect on growth (Table 1.1). Still,

a restraining effect on growth (Table 1.1). Still, the global economy is likely to emerge unscathed in 2024 from the tightening of financial
conditions, with economic growth set to be become gradually more broad-based over the
year. Overall, growth is set to improve to 3.3% in 2025 as central banks cautiously begin to
normalise monetary policy.

Disinflation in the AEs appears to have faltered

The last leg of the disinflation journey is likely to be bumpy
The key deflationary impulses from last year are set to fade. First, energy deflation will
no longer be the main driver of disinflation this year. The fall in energy prices last year
contributed to around one-fifth of the moderation in inflation across the G3. However, oil
prices are expected to average higher this year.1 Moreover, the global oil market is likely to

ear.1 Moreover, the global oil market is likely to experience spurts of volatility amid ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and the
prolonged Russia-Ukraine War.
Second, core goods prices are unlikely to decline by the same extent as they did in 2023.
The earlier unwinding of global supply chain dislocations has largely been completed, and in
turn, Producer Price Indices (PPIs) have picked up recently (Chart 1.7). Meanwhile, the Red
Sea disruptions to shipping now look likely to keep logistical costs elevated for at least the

to keep logistical costs elevated for at least the next few months. This is expected to result in slightly higher core goods prices this year,
particularly in the Eurozone which has faced the largest surge in shipping costs.
Third, the global deflationary impulse from China could fade towards the end of the year.
In the absence of further RMB depreciation, as well as the gradual recovery of the Chinese
economy, China’s PPI is expected to return to positive later this year.

significant step down from the peak of 6% in early-2022.
Productivity has also picked up strongly in recent quarters in the US and is growing well
above its historical average. However, with nominal wage growth still exceeding average
productivity growth by 2% points, this is hindering the return of core inflation back towards

indering the return of core inflation back towards targets (Chart 1.8). In the Eurozone, which is not benefiting from a rebound in productivity as
seen in the US, and where negotiated wage outcomes remain high, hitting inflation targets
will be a greater challenge.
Persistent inflation leading to a delay in policy rate cuts is a significant risk
Against this backdrop, the balance of risks appears to be tilted towards higher inflation.
Disappointing productivity growth, the re-emergence of supply-side disruptions due to an

re-emergence of supply-side disruptions due to an escalation in geopolitical tensions and/or extreme weather events, could cause the current
upward inflation momentum in the AEs to be sustained. A significant widening of conflict in
the Middle East could disrupt oil supply and commercial shipping along the Red Sea and the
Strait of Hormuz, which together account for 30% of global container traffic and nearly 40%
of global seaborne traded oil. Persistently elevated inflation implies interest rates remaining

levated inflation implies interest rates remaining higher for longer, even as the prospect of a broadening conflict in the Middle East triggers
financial market volatility and heightened risk aversion.
If real interest rates remain restrictive for a prolonged period, this may hasten the
crystallisation of latent financial vulnerabilities, resulting in stagflationary-like conditions
across AEs. Softer external demand and higher AE yields would leave several EM Asian

and higher AE yields would leave several EM Asian economies, especially those with negative interest rate differentials with the US, vulnerable
to swings in foreign investment and capital outflows. In turn, persistent exchange rate
depreciation and increased volatility could warrant FX intervention, interest rate hikes, and
even more concerted fiscal consolidation to instil greater confidence in EM assets.
The Chinese economic outlook is also not without risks, given the structural challenges

not without risks, given the structural challenges facing the property sector and local government debt. A sharper-than-expected slowdown in
China would compound the weakness in global economic growth, especially for Asian
economies possessing strong trade linkages with China, via lower imports as well as weaker
global commodity prices. A more bearish outlook for China would likely exacerbate capital
outflows and depreciation pressures across the region. Serendipitously, since 2020, the

to the regional outlook (Chart 1.10).

2 The Singapore Economy
• Singapore’s economic growth eased sequentially in Q1 2024 after
gathering pace in the latter half of 2023. There were two opposing
transitory impulses at play during the quarter. While the tourism-related
industries received some boost from an unprecedented influx of
concerts, the economy was dampened by a pullback in the manufacturing
sector following an electronics-led surge late last year.

following an electronics-led surge late last year. • These two factors influencing the Q1 outcome—the tourism industry pick
up and manufacturing sector pullback—are likely temporary in nature and
should dissipate in the coming quarters. A generally benign global
backdrop, with broad alignment of the macroeconomic, tech and interest
rate cycles, should enable the Singapore economy to attain growth of 1–
3% in 2024, following the 1.1% expansion in 2023.
• For the year as a whole, growth in the trade-related and modern services

e, growth in the trade-related and modern services clusters are expected to improve from 2023, while that in the travel-
related and domestic-oriented clusters will continue to moderate but stay
above trend. Manufacturing and trade activity in Singapore should
continue to benefit from the ongoing recovery in the global electronics
industry, especially in the memory chip segment. Further, the projected
peaking of global policy interest rates is expected to lead to some
rebalancing of investment portfolios, which will in turn bolster financial

t portfolios, which will in turn bolster financial sector activity. Meanwhile, growth in the tourism-related industries
should taper from the concert-driven boost in Q1. Nonetheless, growth
will still be underpinned by upcoming events of a smaller scale, as well as
the continued recovery of Chinese tourists following the removal of
remaining travel frictions.
• This chapter also includes an analysis of Singapore’s long-term growth
path amid business cost developments. While Singapore has managed

ess cost developments. While Singapore has managed to achieve strong economic growth without commensurate cost
increases over the years, it will become increasingly challenging to stay
on this benign course, as the operating environment becomes more
supply-constrained in the coming years. Some within-sector
consolidation is expected in response to the resulting cost increases, but
Singapore can remain globally competitive through continued
technological progress and total factor productivity growth.

cal progress and total factor productivity growth. The Singapore Economy 17
2.1 Recent Economic Developments
The Singapore economy saw a shift in sectoral performances at the start of
2024, impacted by two transitory forces
The soft patch experienced by the Singapore economy in 2023 turned out to be relatively
brief and shallow, lasting only about two quarters in the first half of the year. Growth in the
economy gathered pace towards the end of 2023, amid a pickup in external demand. Overall

of 2023, amid a pickup in external demand. Overall GDP expanded by 1.2% q-o-q SA in Q4, a slight acceleration from the 1.0% growth in Q3 (Chart
2.1). On a y-o-y basis, the pace doubled to 2.2%. The strong Q4 outturn was driven by the
manufacturing and financial sectors, enabling the broader trade-related and modern services
sectors to recoup recent output losses.

However, Q1 2024 witnessed some easing in the momentum, with GDP growth slowing
down to 0.1% q-o-q SA based on the Advance Estimates, even as it picked up to 2.7% in y-o-y
terms on account of the low base in Q1 last year (Chart 2.1). There was a switch of fortunes
among the broad clusters of activity: the trade-related and modern services clusters recorded

rade-related and modern services clusters recorded sequential contractions in Q1 after growing in Q4, while the domestic-oriented and travel-
related clusters expanded in Q1, reversing the declines in the previous quarter (Chart 2.2).
Two opposing, and likely transitory, impulses were at play. On one hand, there was some
correction in the manufacturing sector following an electronics-led surge at the end of 2023.
On the other hand, Singapore benefited from an unprecedented concert bonanza, which

fited from an unprecedented concert bonanza, which provided a boost to the tourism-related industries (i.e., the travel-related cluster as well as the
consumer-facing segments within the domestic-oriented cluster).

There was a pullback in manufacturing activity in Q1, weighed down by the
electronics and biomedicals clusters
The manufacturing sector contracted in Q1 2024 based on the Advance Estimates,
reversing the 4.5% expansion in the previous quarter. On a y-o-y basis, the Index of Industrial
Production (IIP) expanded by 2.1% in Jan–Feb, driven by the transport engineering and
chemicals clusters (Chart 2.3). However, the biomedicals and electronics clusters posted

r, the biomedicals and electronics clusters posted weak growth. Output in the biomedicals cluster was weighed down by a decline in
pharmaceutical production, on account of a different mix of active ingredients being
produced. Meanwhile, electronics output contracted in Jan–Feb in both y-o-y terms as well
as relative to Q4, due to a pullback in semiconductor production. The correction in the first
two months of the year likely reflected some short-term fluctuations in output, as the

ted some short-term fluctuations in output, as the underlying improvement in the global tech cycle remains broadly intact.

Activity in the modern services cluster was primarily dampened by the finance
and insurance sector
The modern services cluster contracted by 4.2% q-o-q SA in Q1 2024, partly reversing the

by 4.2% q-o-q SA in Q1 2024, partly reversing the 4.4% expansion in the previous quarter, with weaker performances across finance &
insurance, professional services and information & communications. On a y-o-y basis, growth
was positive across all three sectors, but there was some moderation for financial services.
The slowdown stemmed from the insurance segment; specifically general insurers reported
a decline in net premiums compared to the strong gain in Q4. Payments processing activity

he strong gain in Q4. Payments processing activity also fell as households brought forward spending to the last quarter of 2023 before the GST

rates remained elevated. Overall loans to residents expanded by 1.0% in February relative to

residents expanded by 1.0% in February relative to end-Q4, picking up from the 0.8% growth in Q3. Loans to non-residents, however, were weak
and fell sequentially by 0.6% in February, extending the decline in the previous two quarters,
dragged down by contractions in lending to the Americas and East Asia regions (Charts 2.6
and 2.7).

Large-scale concerts helped bolster the tourism-related industries
While the economy was weighed down by the manufacturing and financial sectors in Q1,
it benefited from a surge in tourism activity associated with concerts held by internationally

y associated with concerts held by internationally renowned artists Coldplay (in January) and Taylor Swift (in March). These concerts took place
over twelve days in total, with more than half the audience likely coming from abroad. Indeed,
international visitor arrivals rose by 26% in Q1 this year compared to the previous quarter, and
were up a significant 50% compared to the same period last year.
Private sector analysts have estimated the tourism receipts generated by the recent

mated the tourism receipts generated by the recent large-scale concerts in Singapore to range between $350–450 million, depending on the
assumptions made, such as the proportion of foreign visitors among the concertgoers and
the length of stay in Singapore. This represents a 6% injection above the average quarterly
tourism receipts recorded in Q1–Q3 last year, and would have provided a fillip to the travel-
related and domestic-oriented industries such as air transport, accommodation, arts,

stries such as air transport, accommodation, arts, entertainment & recreation, retail and F&B. Based on the 2023 tourism receipts data,
accommodation, sightseeing, entertainment & gaming, F&B and shopping each accounted for
about 15–20% of total receipts (Chart 2.8).
Higher-frequency data showed that these large-scale concerts led to some pickup in
activity in the travel-related and consumer-facing industries. Hotel occupancies rose to 80%
in Jan–Feb this year, compared to 77% in the previous quarter (Chart 2.9). In March, Singapore

previous quarter (Chart 2.9). In March, Singapore hotels and airlines reportedly saw a 30% spike in regional demand around the dates of Swift’s
concerts.1 Retail and F&B sales also increased by 2.1% and 1.1%, respectively in Jan–Feb
relative to Q4, and are expected to have risen further in March, a turnaround from the
contractions recorded in the previous quarter (Chart 2.10).

2.2 DOMESTIC OUTLOOK
Singapore’s economic growth should continue apace for the rest of 2024,
against generally supportive global demand
In the coming quarters, the dual factors acting on the Singapore economy in Q1 are likely
to dissipate. Electronics production should be supported by the continued upturn in the global
tech cycle, while tourism-related activity normalises after the concert-induced windfall. For

normalises after the concert-induced windfall. For the rest of the year, the Singapore economy will likely be driven by a broad alignment of the
global macroeconomic, tech and interest rate cycles (Chart 2.11). The global economy has
continued to display a higher-than-expected resilience and appears to be headed for a “soft
landing”. In the absence of a discernible pullback in economic activity among its major trading
partners, Singapore’s short-term prospects will be underpinned by the upturn in the global

ts will be underpinned by the upturn in the global tech cycle, which is expected to be supported by both cyclical and structural demand. To a
lesser extent, the expected start of the reversal of global monetary policy tightening will also
provide a fillip to the domestic economy.
Overall, Singapore’s GDP growth is projected to come in at 1–3% in 2024, following the
1.1% growth last year, with the main clusters of the economy converging towards their pre-
pandemic growth rates. Growth in the trade-related and modern services clusters are

the trade-related and modern services clusters are expected to improve from last year, while that in the travel-related and domestic-oriented
clusters will continue to moderate but stay above trend.

The global electronics and tech cycle upturn should lend support to the trade-
related cluster
Singapore’s trade-related sectors should continue to benefit from the improved global
trade outlook this year. The WTO forecasts global merchandise trade to expand by 3.3% in
2024, up from 0.8% in 2023. In tandem, ESG expects Singapore’s nominal non-oil domestic
exports to increase by 4.0–6.0% in 2024, a turnaround from the 13.1% contraction last year.

a turnaround from the 13.1% contraction last year. The recovery in global goods trade this year will be buttressed by the cyclical upturn in the
global tech sector. Global chip sales (GCS) rose by 14.9% y-o-y in Jan–Feb, extending the
11.6% expansion in Q4 2023 (Chart 2.12), and marking the sixth consecutive month of y-o-y
gains. Firms in the memory and fabless segments saw higher revenues in Q4, supported by a
nascent pickup in the PC and smartphone end markets, alongside sustained strength in AI-

e end markets, alongside sustained strength in AI- related demand (Chart 2.13). The industry is now on the cusp of an "expansion" phase (Chart
2.12), characterised by positive chip sales growth and a fall in chip inventory. In particular, the
inventory glut which plagued the industry from H2 2022 to H1 2023 has generally eased, with
sporadic chip shortages seen in some end markets facing stronger demand (e.g., AI and
automotive). Since the latter half of 2023, memory chip prices have picked up alongside
gradual inventory restocking (Chart 2.14).

In the coming quarters, the industry’s recovery will be supported by both cyclical and
structural factors. The cyclical upturn is projected to continue, with industry association
World Semiconductor Trade Statistics (WSTS) forecasting 44.8% growth for memory and
6.5% growth for non-memory chip sales this year. Market intelligence firms are expecting

this year. Market intelligence firms are expecting resilient corporate spending on data centre systems and a modest rise in unit shipments of
smartphones and PCs in 2024, buoyed by new tech refresh cycles of consumers and
enterprises (Chart 2.15). These demand drivers are likely to spur the rebuilding of chip
inventory by original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), thereby supporting global chip
demand.
Additionally, structural demand from generative AI (GenAI)-related applications is

from generative AI (GenAI)-related applications is expected to broaden from AI servers to consumer end devices, with Gartner projecting 22%
of global PC and smartphone shipments to be equipped with AI capabilities this year, up from
10% and 1%, respectively in 2023 (Chart 2.16). Growing demand for graphic processing units
(GPUs) that are used in GenAI applications will not only support fabless chip designers and
their foundry partners, but will also benefit the memory chip segment, as the high-bandwidth

fit the memory chip segment, as the high-bandwidth memory (HBM) content per GPU increases. In comparison, automotive and industrial
chipmakers, which saw resilient demand last year, could see more subdued growth in 2024.
The automotive industry is facing some market saturation this year, as end demand for
electric vehicles is likely to taper after a strong surge in 2023. All in, research houses generally
expect double-digit growth for global chip sales in 2024, barring renewed shocks to the global
economy or from geopolitical tensions.

As global interest rates ease gradually, the financial sector should continue
its recovery
Major central banks are expected to begin cutting interest rates in H2 2024, although the
exact timing and pace of rate cuts remain uncertain. Based on the Federal Reserve’s current
median projections as of March 2024, the Federal funds rate should moderate to 4.6% by end-
2024 from 5.3% in end-2023. Similarly, the S$ Singapore Overnight Rate Overage (SORA) is

the S$ Singapore Overnight Rate Overage (SORA) is likely to average around 3.3% in 2024, down from 3.7% in end-2023 (Chart 2.19).2
Easing financial conditions should lead to some shift in investor portfolios, with a
rotation from “value-centric” investment strategies to a “growth-focused” approach. During
this growth-focused phase, investment portfolios are typically rebalanced towards stocks and
bonds as opposed to less risky cash-equivalent assets. Indeed, the MSCI World Equity Index

valent assets. Indeed, the MSCI World Equity Index and the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Corporate Bond Index have continued to post firm
growth in the first quarter of the year, reflecting the improved sentiment and economic
outlook.
As positive sentiment is expected to be broadly sustained in 2024, the bank and
insurance segments should benefit from higher net commissions and fees. Banks’ net
incomes are set to gain from greater retail franchising of their card and wealth businesses,

l franchising of their card and wealth businesses, thus expanding their consumer base. Meanwhile, fund managers should continue to see
AUMs grow strongly, as Singapore intermediates international capital flows through Asia, as
a regional wealth management hub. The payments segment will also grow apace in tandem
with the broader pickup in tourist and discretionary spending.

2.3 Transiting to Singapore’s Long-term Growth Path
As resource constraints and cost increases become more binding in the years to come,
Singapore will likely have to confront the eventuality of its slower long-run growth path. This
section focuses on how the Singapore economy can adapt to this transition, including the
challenges of rising costs.
Singapore has overcome factor constraints to grow strongly over the years

factor constraints to grow strongly over the years Singapore’s economic journey has been characterised by stellar growth and
development over the past few decades, notwithstanding the constraints on labour and
natural resources. (Chart 2.23). Over the period from 1990 to 2023, GDP growth averaged
5.5%, which was in the top 10% of the global growth distribution. This is despite the economy
contending with multiple shocks, such as the Asian Financial Crisis in 1998, Global IT

h as the Asian Financial Crisis in 1998, Global IT Downturn in 2001, Global Financial Crisis in 2009 and the more recent COVID-19 pandemic.
Singapore’s real GDP per capita performed similarly well, rising steadily and significantly
exceeding that of many developed countries (Chart 2.24).

Singapore has managed to achieve an exceptional pace of growth through a
combination of strong capital accumulation and to a lesser extent, total factor productivity
(TFP) gains (Chart 2.25 and 2.26). On the former, there has been a shift in the profile of
Singapore’s capital stock towards information and communications technology (ICT) capital

mation and communications technology (ICT) capital assets since 2010. Notably, ICT capital contributed almost 50% to Singapore’s real GDP
growth in 2020–23, double the global average and broadly comparable to other advanced
economies like the US (Chart 2.27). The accumulation of ICT capital was most evident in the
modern services cluster, accounting for 41% of its VA in 2022 (Chart 2.28). While the
contribution of ICT capital was relatively lower in the trade-related cluster at 11%, its growth
rate was the fastest among the three broad clusters.

almost a quarter to Singapore’s real GDP growth in 2020–23, double that of the global average

The firm pace of GDP growth in Singapore has so far taken place alongside a measured

gapore has so far taken place alongside a measured increase in business costs. Since 2010, overall average monthly earnings have risen by close
to 60% (Chart 2.31). However, the improvement in labour productivity has partially mitigated
the rise in wage costs, with unit labour cost (ULC) growing at half the pace at 30%. This was
especially the case in the trade-related and modern services sectors, which saw more
moderate ULC increases compared to the non-tradable sector. Meanwhile, the unit business

non-tradable sector. Meanwhile, the unit business cost (UBC) for the overall economy, was estimated to have risen by a larger 65% over the past
decade (Chart 2.32). The modern services and trade-related clusters recorded stronger
increases of 55% and 75% in UBC respectively, compared to 25% in the non-tradable sector.
It is likely that these cost increases were not directly associated with domestic factor
constraints but reflected, in part, the rise in international prices. For instance, freight and

in international prices. For instance, freight and transport charges drove the increase in costs in the trade-related sector. During the COVID-
19 pandemic, supply chain disruptions significantly impacted the shipping industry, with
lockdowns, container shortages and port congestions adding to freight costs.

The global economy is likely to face several cost headwinds in the coming years, as
growth becomes increasingly supply-constrained alongside population ageing, declining
labour supply and changes in working preferences as economies move up the income ladder.
Accordingly, import prices could rise as Singapore’s trading partners grapple with these
supply issues. There are also costs associated with geoeconomic fragmentation, as firms

ssociated with geoeconomic fragmentation, as firms navigate disruptions to their supply chains by diversifying and establishing new and more
costly input sources. Meanwhile, climate change action also entails large public and private
sector investments to facilitate the energy transition, which will eventually translate to higher
input prices. On the domestic front, labour costs and ULC growth are expected to trend up in
the coming years, especially for lower-productivity sectors where labour constraints are the
tightest.

sectors where labour constraints are the
tightest. It is crucial that Singapore maintains a positive cycle where high wages
correspond to high productivity levels
Countries with high productivity in the tradable sector tend to have high wages and
prices in the non-tradable sector compared to countries with lower productivity. However, this
phenomenon (termed the “Samuelson-Balassa hypothesis”) has been somewhat more muted
in Singapore, with wages and prices in the non-tradable sector remaining significantly lower

non-tradable sector remaining significantly lower than those in the tradable sector thus far, reflecting in part the fairly elastic foreign worker
supply in the 2000s. Going forward, as labour constraints become more binding, wages and
prices would likely have to rise more quickly relative to other countries, to more accurately
reflect the true resource costs of producing essential non-tradable goods and services,
against growing demand.
Singapore’s tradable sector as a whole should be able to cope with rising costs, provided

should be able to cope with rising costs, provided firms continue to move towards the higher value-added parts of the global value chain. Over
the last decade, Singapore’s share in the world’s total goods exports has remained stable at
around 2%. Meanwhile, Singapore’s share in global services exports grew from 2.5% to 4.1%,
which was mainly driven by the water transport and finance & insurance sectors. With more
binding resource constraints, firms in the tradable sectors will have to optimise their factor

radable sectors will have to optimise their factor inputs with technology solutions to a greater extent than before. There could be some shifts
in the composition of the tradable sectors, towards industries that can command a premium
in global markets. For instance, FDI has been channelled into expanding the ICT and
semiconductor industries in recent years, leveraging on Singapore’s ability to adapt to fast-
moving technology trends. These industries exhibited one of the highest annualised FDI

stries exhibited one of the highest annualised FDI growth rates between 2010 to 2022 of about 15%, compared to 12% for the overall economy.
In comparison, some firms in the non-tradable sector may be less able to pass on higher
costs to consumers. Chart 2.34 illustrates gross operating surplus (GOS) as a share of
revenue by industry in 2022, and how it has evolved over the last ten years. Firms in sectors
in the bottom left of the chart, characterised by the lowest GOS as a share of revenue, and

rised by the lowest GOS as a share of revenue, and experiencing the most significant decline over the past decade, are at greater risk of
consolidation as companies face rising costs. These include the domestic-oriented sectors
such as retail and F&B. However, these sectors need not necessarily shrink in importance.
Rather, the non-tradable corporate landscape could see compositional shifts, where
businesses prioritise offering higher quality, differentiated goods and services, aligning with

, differentiated goods and services, aligning with the evolving, more sophisticated consumption patterns emerging in the domestic market,
alongside the general rise in wages.

estic market,
alongside the general rise in wages. 34 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024

Singapore will have to confront the eventuality of long-term growth driven by

ront the eventuality of long-term growth driven by TFP gains
Higher business costs do not necessarily preclude a country from being competitive.
Higher wages that are typically a result of productivity growth enabled by technological
progress, can place the economy in a virtuous circle.3 This phenomenon is borne out in Chart
2.35. where it can be seen that countries with higher wages also tend to be highly competitive
and excel in several domains from business dynamism to innovation and capability. In a

siness dynamism to innovation and capability. In a sense, higher costs accompanied by higher productivity and incomes can strengthen broader
economic competitiveness, economic depth and societal welfare.
3 See for example, De Grauwe, P (2017), The Limits of the Market: The Pendulum between Government and Market, Oxford

3 Labour Market and Inflation
• Employment growth moderated across all broad sectors of the economy
in Q4 last year, bringing the labour market closer into balance. Slowing
labour demand in most sectors, along with the fading of bonus effects, led
to a moderation in aggregate resident wage growth from earlier high rates.
• In the first half of this year, employment growth is likely to remain modest

year, employment growth is likely to remain modest before the anticipated pickup in VA growth later this year provides some
support. Despite the relatively subdued growth in labour demand projected
for 2024 as a whole, persistent labour supply constraints in certain
services sectors imply that there is unlikely to be any significant uptick in
labour market slack. Consequently, the resident unemployment rate
should stay close to its long-term historical average.
• Weaker labour demand in some external-facing sectors of the economy,

nd in some external-facing sectors of the economy, combined with last year’s high base effects from atypically large bonuses,
should more than offset wage pressures in the domestic-oriented services
sectors. Overall, growth in average monthly earnings should slow to
around the pre-COVID norm in 2024. Alongside an improvement in labour
productivity, unit labour cost increases should ease this year.
• MAS Core Inflation was unchanged in Q1 from Q4, coming in lower than
expected. Inflation for food and travel-related services fell markedly, and

ood and travel-related services fell markedly, and broadly offset faster increases in electricity & gas prices arising from the
carbon tax hike, and in other services costs. A further moderation in private
transport and accommodation inflation also contributed to a decline in
CPI-All Items inflation. Overall, underlying inflation in the economy
remained on a broad downward trajectory, in line with easing business
cost pressures. This underlying disinflation trend is affirmed by a range of
alternative core inflation measures.

by a range of
alternative core inflation measures. • MAS Core Inflation is forecast to decline gradually over the course of the
year but will only step down more discernibly in Q4 and into 2025. In the
near term, core inflation will remain at around current levels reflecting the
hike in water prices and further expected adjustments in services prices to
catch up to earlier cost increases. However, as imported and domestic
cost pressures continue to ease, the disinflation trend should continue. For

ease, the disinflation trend should continue. For 2024 as a whole, both MAS Core Inflation and CPI-All Items inflation are
