3.1 Labour Market1
Labour demand slowed across all broad clusters last year
Total employment growth softened across all broad clusters in the economy in Q4 last
year, as labour demand cooled (Chart 3.1). The moderation in employment growth was mainly
on account of slower net inflows of non-resident workers, with resident employment
remaining broadly stable.
Notably, employment in the modern services2 and trade-related clusters contracted for

ervices2 and trade-related clusters contracted for the first time since Q3 2020 and Q4 2021, respectively, reflecting a pullback in hiring and
uptick in retrenchments (Chart 3.2). Other segments of the economy also saw dampened
employment growth in Q4 last year. Amid weaker sales volumes, the typical year-end
seasonal employment boost in the F&B and retail trade sectors was more muted than
anticipated. Overall, total employment growth moderated in line with the cyclical weakness in
GDP growth over most of 2023 (Chart 3.3).

In the second half of the year, the anticipated pickup in GDP growth should boost labour
demand modestly and provide some support to employment growth towards the end of
2024.6 With the projected easing of monetary policy by central banks, improvements in the
global growth outlook should lead to a moderate increase in hiring for the external-facing
modern services sectors, while firm inbound tourism should support demand for workers in
consumer-facing services.

rt demand for workers in
consumer-facing services. All in, total employment growth is expected to moderate this year, relative to the
exceptionally strong growth over the past two years, which was largely driven by the catch-up
in non-resident workforce numbers to pre-COVID levels. Employment growth this year should
be more in line with its pre-COVID trend.
Even as labour demand moderates this year, supply constraints will keep the
labour market close to full employment

ll keep the
labour market close to full employment Resident labour supply will likely be constrained by limits to further increases in the trend
LFPR. The dampening impact of the ageing population on LFPR will increasingly outweigh
structural factors boosting labour force participation, such as longer life expectancy and
higher educational attainment. Labour policies could nevertheless mitigate the negative
impact of population ageing somewhat by further lifting participation rates of women and

y further lifting participation rates of women and older workers. See Box A for an empirical analysis of the structural drivers of labour force
participation in Singapore.
In the short term, worker shortages could persist in parts of the economy this year due
to the limited capacity of employers to add to their non-resident workforce, especially for
sectors that face difficulties in attracting resident workers. Following strong growth of non-
resident employment in the services sector over the past two years, a larger number of firms

over the past two years, a larger number of firms are likely to be facing binding dependency ratio ceilings for increasing their work permit holder
or S-Pass holder headcounts, relative to the past two years.
Amid structural constraints on labour supply, the resident unemployment rate is not
expected to rise sharply beyond its pre-COVID average, even as labour market tightness
dissipates. In addition, the anticipated cyclical upswing later this year should help offset any

cal upswing later this year should help offset any increase in labour market slack. However, job seekers could face greater challenges finding
employment if they are unable or unwilling to move into sectors where job vacancy rates
remain elevated. These labour market mismatch dynamics could lead to some increase in
unemployment even as worker shortages persist. Alternatively, the mismatch could lead to
some decline in LFPR, if some unemployed individuals become discouraged and exit the
labour force.

Box A: Drivers of Labour Force Participation in Singapore
Introduction
Singapore’s current resident labour force participation rate (LFPR) of 68.6% in 2023 is
relatively high, comparable to those of global leaders, such as Switzerland and New Zealand,
and 7.7% points above the OECD (2022) average of 60.9%. However, further increases in
overall LFPR may be limited, due to a combination of continued population ageing, and the

ombination of continued population ageing, and the slowing growth of LFPR for females and older residents. Slowing LFPR growth could
constrain further resident workforce expansion, which may consequently contribute less to
the economy’s potential growth.
This Box analyses the historical structural drivers of labour force participation of
different age-gender groups in Singapore, using a panel regression approach. Structural
drivers identified include changing life expectancy, educational attainment, and patterns in

xpectancy, educational attainment, and patterns in family formation. A better understanding of the contribution of structural factors to LFPR
movements could help shed light on effective policy levers for boosting labour force
participation.
The analysis finds that the two biggest contributors to the rise in Singapore’s overall
trend LFPR from 2003 to 2023 were longer life expectancies and higher educational
attainment. These likely boosted the participation rate of women in particular. Looking ahead,

pation rate of women in particular. Looking ahead, trend LFPR is likely to plateau as the ageing population profile will increasingly outweigh the
boost from rising life expectancy and educational attainment, with further improvements in
these two areas likely to be limited.
Overview of Recent LFPR Trends
The overall LFPR for the resident population has risen steadily over the past two decades,
from 63.2% in 2003 to 68.6% in 2023. The two groups that have contributed most to the overall

o groups that have contributed most to the overall LFPR increase have been i) females, as well as ii) older workers. Two further observations are
notable.
• LFPR has risen significantly for females while remaining broadly stable for males (Charts
A1 and A2). The rise in LFPR for females was observed across all age groups, except the
youngest age cohort, i.e., aged 15–24, as a larger proportion of them pursue further
education.
• Over the years, LFPR has risen sharply for both males and females aged 55 and above.

rply for both males and females aged 55 and above. While the LFPR increase for males aged 55–64 eased in the 2010s, LFPR for females in
this age group continued to rise strongly.

ales in
this age group continued to rise strongly. 44 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024

Estimating Drivers of LFPR
To explain the change in LFPR trends within age-gender groups, a panel regression

rends within age-gender groups, a panel regression approach, adapted from a Brookings study by Aaronson et al. (2014), is used to estimate the
contributions of cyclical and structural drivers. This step estimates how trend participation
rates within age-gender groups have changed over time due to structural factors, while
remaining agnostic to the changes in the population’s age structure over time.

1. Educational attainment (as measured by the proportion of population in each age-gender
group with tertiary education of diploma & above): Higher educational attainment has
been found to be associated with greater labour force participation, as more educated
individuals have greater earnings potential (OECD, 2023). However, research has also

potential (OECD, 2023). However, research has also found that some more highly educated individuals may exit the labour force at a younger

et al., 2019).
2. Life expectancy (defined as the average remaining life expectancy for each age-gender
group): Life expectancy has been found to be positively correlated with labour force
participation, as more working years will be required to accumulate sufficient savings

for retirement. A European Central Bank study (Balleer et al., 2009) of five Euro area

3. Marriage rate (defined as the proportion of the population in each age-gender group that
is married) and average number of children per female (refers to the average number of

ildren per female (refers to the average number of children born to each female age group): The literature has generally found that prime
working-age women are less likely to work when they are married with young children
than single mothers (e.g., Azcona et al., 2020), suggesting that childbearing and
childcare responsibilities have a stronger negative association with labour force
participation for married women. This is likely because married women take on more
homemaking tasks and cede income-seeking responsibilities to men. A marriage

income-seeking responsibilities to men. A marriage rate-female dummy interaction term in the specification accounts for the effects of
marriage, while another interaction term, consisting of average number of children per
thousand women and a childbearing female dummy, is included to account for the
effects of childbearing on labour force participation among women.
4. Gender wage ratio (defined as male earnings/female earnings5): A large gender wage
ratio (i.e., a larger gap between male and female wages) reduces the opportunity cost of

and female wages) reduces the opportunity cost of home production, such as housework and childcare, for females, vis-à-vis males, and
incentivises women to engage in home production rather than join the labour force. An
IMF study by Kinoshita and Guo (2015) found that a large gender wage ratio (i.e., higher
relative male wages) is associated with lower female employment in formal work and
higher female employment in the informal sector. A gender wage ratio-female dummy
interaction term is included in the specification.

interaction term is included in the specification. 5. Time trend: In the Singapore context, some of the increase in trend LFPR for older
individuals likely reflects a combination of policy moves to encourage senior workers to
remain in the labour force, including shifts in retirement and re-employment age policies6,
as well as possibly greater embedded preferences for work among newer cohorts of
older workers. Due to the econometric challenges of accounting for each of these effects

challenges of accounting for each of these effects separately, a time trend is introduced for certain age-gender groups to flexibly control for
the combined effects of policy and preference shifts.7
Overall, the empirical strategy utilises variation in structural factors among age-gender
cohorts across time to estimate drivers of the aggregate resident LFPR. As the validity of this
strategy relies on the assumption that the structural drivers are exogenously determined, only

tructural drivers are exogenously determined, only variables that can be reasonably expected to be independent are included as potential drivers
in the model. For example, educational attainment and life expectancies have likely increased
across time largely due to the development of Singapore’s schooling and medical
infrastructure, and are likely to be exogenous.

Estimation Results
The estimation results suggest that cyclical factors (as measured by the LMPI), life
expectancy, educational attainment, marriage, average number of children (per thousand
females), as well as the gender wage ratio were significant drivers of labour force
participation in Singapore, largely in line with the literature. The estimated coefficients of the
selected model are presented under “Specification (1)” in Table A1.8

Some of the key results and insights from this estimation exercise are as follows:
1. Consistent with empirical literature, higher educational attainment and life expectancies
are associated with higher LFPR in Singapore. The effect of educational attainment and

force by 11.6% on average9, while a 1% point increase in the average share of population

2. Increasing the average number of children (per thousand females) by one is associated
with a 1% decline in the likelihood of a woman of childbearing age participating in the
labour force, suggesting that childbearing discourages labour force participation by
women in Singapore.

women in Singapore. 3. Marriage is found to be positively associated with participating in the labour force (as
indicated by the positive coefficient on the marriage variable), suggesting that the larger
financial commitments associated with marriage prompt stronger labour force
attachment. However, marriage improves labour force participation for females to a
smaller extent, relative to males (as indicated by the negative coefficient on the

s (as indicated by the negative coefficient on the interaction term between marriage and a female dummy). An increase in marriage rate
by 1% point only raises the likelihood of labour force participation of females by 4%,
around half of its impact on the male labour force participation.
4. A 1% point decline in the gender wage ratio is associated with a 1.3% increase in the
likelihood of labour force participation for females. This suggests that reducing gender
wage inequality could help to encourage labour force participation among women and

courage labour force participation among women and boost overall LFPR.
5. The positive coefficient on the LMPI implies that LFPR is generally procyclical in
Singapore, similar to countries like the US (Krusell et al., 2017). That is, there is typically
a net inflow of workers into the resident labour force during periods of cyclical labour
market tightness when labour demand and wage growth tend to be strong, while more
workers exit the labour force when there is significant labour market slack.

Decomposition of Historical Change in LFPR As shown by the decomposition in Chart A5, the largest positive contributors to
increases in trend LFPR from 2003 to 2023 were higher life expectancies and rising education
levels. The share of the population aged 25 and above with a diploma qualification and above
more than doubled from 25.8% in 2003 to 53.1% in 2022 (Chart A6), while the average life
expectancy rose from 79.1 to 83 years over the same period.11 In comparison, the fall in

over the same period.11 In comparison, the fall in marriage rates contributed to some decline in trend LFPR. However, for females, the negative
effect of declining marriage rates on LFPR was partly counteracted by a falling fertility rate,
which encouraged more women to participate in the workforce. Finally, the decline in gender
wage ratio, as well as the time trend terms representing policy and preference shifts among
older individuals have also boosted overall LFPR.

Overall, trend LFPR rose by 3.6% points from 64% in 2003 to 67.6% in 2023. The structural
forces pushing up LFPR within age-gender groups over the last 20 years raised trend LFPR by
12.6% points, which more than offset the drag from an ageing population and declining
marriage rates (−5.8% points and −3.2% points respectively).
Conclusion

points and −3.2% points respectively).
Conclusion Looking forward, the drag on LFPR caused by the underlying ageing population profile is
expected to increasingly outweigh the boost from structural factors, primarily as rising life
expectancies and educational attainment, which were the largest drivers of historical
increases in trend LFPR, are already plateauing. If current trends continue, trend LFPR may
fall gradually in the future.
To facilitate some continued growth in “within-group” LFPRs, the estimation results

th in “within-group” LFPRs, the estimation results affirm the focus of current policy efforts on two areas. First, engendering greater labour force
attachment among seniors will be important to facilitate a continued increase in overall LFPR.
Given that newer cohorts of seniors have higher average educational attainment and incomes
relative to their predecessors, their participation rates could be higher than implied by the
projections based on panel regression results. Efforts to improve productivity and skills,

sults. Efforts to improve productivity and skills, strengthen employment facilitation support, enhance employers’ abilities to create
age-friendly jobs and workplaces, and shift mindsets of workers and employers would help
encourage seniors to extend their time in the workforce and employers to hire seniors.
Second, the estimates suggest that having children and getting married are still
correlated with lower labour force participation for women, relative to men. This implies that

MAS Core Inflation averaged 3.3% y-o-y in Q1 2024, unchanged from Q4 2023 and slightly
lower than expected. Inflation for food and travel-related services slowed markedly and
broadly offset the steeper pace of price increases for electricity & gas as well as other
services (Chart 3.9). Meanwhile, private transport and accommodation inflation fell and

ate transport and accommodation inflation fell and contributed to a discernible decline in CPI-All Items inflation to 3.0% y-o-y, from 4.0% in Q4
2023.
Excluding the impact of the GST increase in January this year, core inflation would have
been lower in Q1. The GST hike to 9% has likely been fully passed through to most consumer
prices as of March. While some residual pass-through remains due to several retailers’
decisions to temporarily absorb the GST increase until later in 20241, this is unlikely to raise

se until later in 20241, this is unlikely to raise inflation significantly in the upcoming months.

The underlying disinflation process remains intact

The underlying disinflation process remains intact For a more holistic assessment of the general trend of inflation, alternative measures of
underlying inflation are used to distinguish persistent, “core” price movements from price
changes that are driven by more temporary disturbances. Such measures continue to indicate
disinflation in domestic consumer prices: the 25% trimmed mean inflation slowed to 3.0%
y-o-y in Q1 2024 from 3.8% in Q4 2023, while the weighted median inflation moderated to 3.0%

le the weighted median inflation moderated to 3.0% from 3.7% over the same period (Chart 3.14).6 The volatility-adjusted measure of core
inflation fell to 3.3%, from 3.7%. These suggest that the bulk of price changes in the CPI
remained on an easing path thus far this year and underlying inflation is kept elevated only by
a narrow set of items experiencing large increases. This is in line with the leftward shift in the
distribution of price increases (Chart 3.15). Notably, the share of core CPI basket registering

in some occupations and sectors continue to adjust to clear persistent labour supply-demand
mismatches. Nonetheless, aggregate wage growth in the economy is expected to moderate
with softer labour market conditions in the external-facing sectors. This should have a
restraining effect on consumer demand, and consequently the ability of businesses to raise
prices strongly.

headline inflation. COE premiums have fallen markedly from 2023 levels, and the resultant
decline in private transport inflation is expected to be the single largest driver behind the fall
in headline inflation this year. Apart from LTA’s intentions to carry forward future guaranteed
de-registrations to more recent bidding rounds, the COE quota is also forecast to continue to

nds, the COE quota is also forecast to continue to increase alongside a rising population of cars reaching the compulsory age for either de-

further as the supply of accommodation continues to rise, while leasing demand could soften
amid slower inflows of non-resident workers.
For 2024 as a whole, both core and headline inflation are projected to average 2.5–3.5%.

Box B: Alternative Measures of Inflation in Singapore
Introduction
Most central banks, with the primary objective of achieving low and stable inflation,
construct and monitor several measures of underlying or core inflation. Such measures
generally aim to tease out persistent, “core” price movements and distinguish these from
price changes driven by more temporary disturbances or “noise”. Policymakers must focus

y disturbances or “noise”. Policymakers must focus on the persistent trend in prices because monetary policy only transmits to prices with a lag;
it would neither be possible nor ideal for monetary policy to respond to erratic changes in a
narrow set of prices. Measures of inflation that capture broad-based, persistent trends in
prices would be better guides for monetary policy. In addition, some measures of core
inflation also explicitly exclude price fluctuations from non-monetary sources so that the

fluctuations from non-monetary sources so that the resultant measure of inflation can be better seen as an outcome of monetary policy. Such
measures may therefore serve as operational targets for which central banks can be held
accountable.
While MAS looks at a broad range of price and cost indicators in the economy, MAS Core
Inflation is the measure that is monitored most closely in formulating monetary policy. MAS
Core Inflation excludes accommodation and private road transport prices, as these

dation and private road transport prices, as these components are often driven by administrative supply-side changes and can be more volatile
than most other components of CPI. At the same time, MAS Core Inflation includes food and
utilities because these prices can be directly influenced by MAS’ exchange rate-based
monetary policy. Food and utilities prices are salient to all households, whereas car prices
and market rents of residential accommodation have little impact on the day-to-day cash
outlays of most Singaporeans.1

the day-to-day cash
outlays of most Singaporeans.1 Central banks have found value in incorporating several measures of core inflation in
their policy analysis and formulation, as no one measure of inflation will be a superior guide
to policy in all circumstances (Ehrmann et al., 2018, Bullard, 2011). The experience of high
inflation over the 2021–23 period, involving a complex combination of supply and demand
shocks whose effects are of varying persistence, highlights the importance of having

g persistence, highlights the importance of having alternative measures of underlying inflation to support policy formulation. This box item
examines a variety of underlying inflation measures that may serve as useful guides to
monetary policy in Singapore.
Alternative Inflation Measures
There are several different approaches to measuring core inflation that are commonly
used internationally (Table B1):
1 A significant share of accommodation costs in the CPI is the cost of “owner-occupied accommodation”, which

the cost of “owner-occupied accommodation”, which is meant to reflect the costs to homeowners of utilising the flow of services provided by their homes. This is
proxied by market rents. However, this indicator has no direct impact on the monthly cash expenditure of most
households as most Singaporeans own their homes and do not pay housing rent. Meanwhile, private road
transport costs are significantly influenced by the prevailing prices of “Certificates of Entitlement” (COEs)

ing prices of “Certificates of Entitlement” (COEs) determined under the vehicle quota system. Again, the majority of households would not be frequently affected
by changes in COE prices as most car buyers have a long interval between car purchases, in line with the 10-
year validity for most COEs.

in line with the 10-
year validity for most COEs. Labour Market and Inflation 59

• Exclusion-based measures. These measures systematically exclude some specific
components of the CPI that are regarded as being highly volatile, reflecting their
susceptibility to supply shocks. This category includes the MAS Core Inflation measure
as well as a commonly used measure of core inflation internationally that removes fresh

core inflation internationally that removes fresh food and energy prices from the CPI.
• Volatility-adjusted (VA) measures. This type of measure increases or decreases CPI
expenditure weights in proportion to the relative volatility of CPI components over a
particular period. The measure constructed for Singapore adjusts the component
weights for the relative variance in 3-month, seasonally adjusted group-level price
changes. Items that were found to be particularly volatile and down-weighted include

be particularly volatile and down-weighted include telecommunications equipment, private transport, other transport services, and utilities
and fuels. Items that have low volatility and are up-weighted include an array of food
components with a high service content (e.g., hawker food and restaurant meals), as
well as personal services items (e.g., holidays and telecommunication services).
• Limited influence measures. These include the weighted median and trimmed-mean

These include the weighted median and trimmed-mean measures, and involve replacing extreme price changes, on both ends, with the weighted
average of the remaining prices, on a period-by-period basis. Unlike the exclusion-based
or VA measures, the items excluded from the calculations vary across time, which makes
them robust to a wide range of shocks. In this analysis, three measures are calculated
based on seasonally adjusted group-level price changes over 3 months: the weighted

up-level price changes over 3 months: the weighted median; a 25% symmetrically trimmed mean; and a 10% symmetrically trimmed mean.
The data used in this analysis have three important characteristics:
• Aggregation. Data used are at the group level of aggregation, which consists of 42
categories of prices in Singapore’s CPI basket. This level of aggregation was selected
for two main reasons. First, it is the most detailed level of aggregation that is publicly
available, so that core inflation measures derived from this can be externally verified.

ures derived from this can be externally verified. Second, the level of aggregation is detailed enough for analytical purposes, but not so
granular that it would complicate policy communication.

lar that it would complicate policy communication. 60 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
• Frequency. The analysis focuses on price changes over 3 months. Price changes over
12 months are relatively smooth but are slow at catching turning points. Month-to-month
price changes catch turning points quickly but are noisy. Accordingly, the 3-month price
changes strike a balance between immediacy and smoothness and facilitate analysis
and communication in conjunction with quarterly GDP data.

munication in conjunction with quarterly GDP data. • Seasonal adjustment. All data used are seasonally adjusted by the Department of
Statistics (DOS).
Historical Behaviour of Alternative Inflation Measures
Table B2 shows average rates of inflation and volatility indicators for the CPI-All Items
(headline), MAS Core and alternative core inflation measures over the period 2004–23. All
inflation series presented in this Box are end-of-period quarterly values, as the information

end-of-period quarterly values, as the information content in these more up-to-date indicators is arguably more useful to policymakers.

below that of the headline CPI rate, but generally only slightly lower than MAS Core.
Among the alternative measures, the average reading for the 10% trimmed mean
measure was closest to the headline rate.
• Most measures of core inflation were significantly less volatile than CPI-All Items

ere significantly less volatile than CPI-All Items inflation. For the headline rate, the volatility of the changes in the quarterly inflation rate
was 0.7% point. MAS Core Inflation and CPI XFE were about 10% less volatile, while the
CPI VA, trimmed mean and weighted median measures were 40–50% less volatile. This
also translated into lower volatility in y-o-y inflation rates.

d relatively stable, and was often included in the trimmed mean measures. Meanwhile, several components, such as fresh food, saw

r trimmed components and the remaining non-trimmed ones. For example, from 2017–18, utilities showed strong price increases compared to

The CPI VA index retains all components in the CPI-All Items index but down-weights
both accommodation and private transport due to their relatively high levels of volatility. It
also down-weights other volatile components, such as utilities, and up-weights CPI
components whose prices move more smoothly.

Overall, instances of divergences between MAS Core Inflation and the alternative core

etween MAS Core Inflation and the alternative core measures can contain useful economic information on the nature of current price increases
and outlook for future inflation. In this regard, recent inflation outturns may provide a useful
case in point.
While all alternative measures had tracked MAS Core Inflation very closely from the
onset of COVID-19, there has been a slight divergence in the most recent data. In Q1 this year,
MAS Core Inflation in y-o-y terms showed only a modest decline of 0.2% point from a quarter

only a modest decline of 0.2% point from a quarter ago, while the 25% trimmed mean and VA inflation fell by a larger extent (0.8% and 0.4% point
respectively). This reflected large price increases for a few components in MAS Core that are
infrequently adjusted, such as public transport and healthcare. Public transport fares are only
adjusted once a year at the end of December, and prices were adjusted in end-2023 to catch
up with earlier cost increases. Consequently, there was a step-up in public transport inflation

there was a step-up in public transport inflation in Q1 2024 even as inflation in most other CPI components continued to ease. The continuing
moderation in the bulk of price changes in the CPI and that of lower-volatility components
suggests that underlying inflation remains on a broad easing trend.
Empirical Assessment of Alternative Inflation Measures
The approach proposed by Cogley (1998) is employed to evaluate the predictive power
of alternative inflation measures on future (“target”) MAS Core Inflation and headline inflation.

arget”) MAS Core Inflation and headline inflation. It assumes that the current deviation of underlying inflation from target inflation (either MAS
Core or CPI All-Items) should be inversely related to subsequent changes in target inflation.
Thus, if a measure indeed captures underlying trend inflation and the target inflation is above
(or below) trend, the target series should correct itself by falling (or rising) over time. The test
uses a regression of the following form:

Two sets of results are presented in this section. The first set configures MAS Core

and provides insights into whether the alternative inflation series contain informational content for predicting MAS Core Inflation. The second

The estimates show that CPI XFE is ineffective in predicting the future path of MAS Core
Inflation (Table B3). This result is not unexpected, as there is little overlap between the
excluded components of CPI XFE and MAS Core. The excluded components themselves are
also unlikely to be driven by the same underlying shocks. Meanwhile, the estimated 𝛽
coefficients for the trimmed mean and CPI VA indices are statistically significant and

d CPI VA indices are statistically significant and negative, though the trimmed mean measures tend to overstate future core inflation while the
CPI VA measure appears to understate it. In terms of their goodness of fit as indicated by the
equation 𝑅2, CPI VA performed best, followed by the weighted median and trimmed mean
measures.

Sum-Up
In conclusion, the analysis in this box suggests that alternative measures of underlying
inflation can help provide a clearer picture of the general trend of inflation. In particular, the
weighted median, trimmed mean and CPI VA measures can help to corroborate trends in MAS
Core Inflation, or alert policymakers to movements in specific components of the MAS Core

o movements in specific components of the MAS Core basket that may be less representative of current inflation. As such, these alternative
measures have a valuable place in the suite of price indicators monitored by MAS for use in
monetary policy formulation and communication.

4 Macroeconomic Policy
• In January and April this year, MAS maintained the prevailing rate of
appreciation of the S$NEER policy band, with no change to the width of the
band or the level at which it was centred. MAS assessed that as inflation
was expected to stay elevated in the immediate quarters ahead, the
prevailing rate of appreciation of the policy band is needed to keep a
restraining effect on imported inflation and domestic cost pressures. The

mported inflation and domestic cost pressures. The gradually appreciating policy band is consistent with inflation stepping
down more discernibly in Q4 and into 2025, and is sufficient for ensuring
medium-term price stability.
• Amid improving economic growth prospects and still-elevated inflation,
Budget 2024 was tailored to provide further assistance to households and
firms facing cost-of-living and cashflow concerns. At the same time, it set
out the Government’s priorities in building a more productivity-driven

priorities in building a more productivity-driven economy and equitable society, in line with the vision laid out in the
Forward Singapore report. Nonetheless, the Budget’s overall fiscal impulse
for CY2024 was estimated to be appropriately restrained in aggregate.
Overall, macroeconomic policy will continue to contribute to medium-term
price stability and sustainable growth in the economy.
4.1 Monetary Policy
In January 2024, MAS maintained the prevailing rate of appreciation of the
S$NEER policy band, with no change to its width and level

policy band, with no change to its width and level At the time of the January 2024 monetary policy review, global economic activity was
more resilient than envisaged, largely reflecting better-than-expected outturns in the US and
China. Overall growth momentum was expected to slow in the near term due to the lagged
impact of high interest rates and less supportive fiscal policy. Global final demand was
expected to pick up later in 2024, as lower inflation would underpin private consumption

lower inflation would underpin private consumption expenditure even as monetary policy settings in the major economies turned more supportive.
The nascent recovery in the global tech and trade cycles would also boost activity in the
regional economies.
The resilience in the global economic environment was reflected in the Singapore
economy which picked up towards the end of 2023 and was projected to continue improving
over the following year. The manufacturing and trade-related services sectors would be

turing and trade-related services sectors would be buoyed by the recovery in the global electronics industry, while activity in financial services
was forecast to pick up in line with the plateauing and eventual easing of global interest rates.
Meanwhile, growth in the domestic-oriented and travel-related clusters would ease, albeit
remain above-trend, this year. Consequently, the slightly negative output gap in 2023 was
expected to narrow in the second half of 2024.

was
expected to narrow in the second half of 2024. MAS Core Inflation had moderated slightly in year-on-year terms in the last quarter of
2023. Core inflation was expected to pick up in Q1 2024 and stay elevated in the earlier part

p in Q1 2024 and stay elevated in the earlier part 66 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
of the year, due in part to the one-off impact of the hike in the GST to 9%, as well as the
increase in the carbon tax, both of which were implemented in January 2024. Meanwhile
water prices were slated to rise from Q2 amid increases in production costs. Inflation in
services components such as healthcare and public transport would also remain elevated, as
services fees in these sectors were projected to continue catching up to earlier increases in
costs.

ontinue catching up to earlier increases in
costs. Looking through the transitory impact of the GST increase, core inflation was forecast
to decline gradually over 2024, underpinned by lower import costs as well as a slower pace
of labour cost increases. Global energy and food commodity prices were expected to remain
broadly stable, while imported costs of most intermediate and final goods would also be
tempered by ample global supply conditions. On the domestic front, wage growth was

conditions. On the domestic front, wage growth was expected to ease this year as labour market tightness dissipated, while productivity was
projected to pick up. Unit labour cost increases were consequently expected to moderate
significantly, which would help alleviate business cost pressures.
With core inflation expected to step down by Q4 2024 and fall further in 2025, MAS
assessed that prevailing monetary policy settings remained appropriate. The sustained
appreciation of the policy band would continue to dampen imported inflation and curb

uld continue to dampen imported inflation and curb domestic cost pressures, thus ensuring medium-term price stability.
Singapore’s economic growth is expected to strengthen over the course of
the year, similar to projections made in January
Global economic activity continued to demonstrate resilience in Q1. Growth in the US
stayed firm, underpinned by a tight labour market which supported consumption. In China,
economic activity picked up from H2 2023, due mainly to stronger exports and manufacturing

, due mainly to stronger exports and manufacturing investment. The relatively sanguine global economic environment in Q1, as well as the
ongoing upturn in the global trade and tech cycles, supported the expansions in the external-
oriented Asian economies, in line with prior expectations.
In the quarters ahead, the global economy should maintain a steady pace of expansion,
although high real interest rates and less supportive fiscal policies will exert a modest drag.

pportive fiscal policies will exert a modest drag. Notably, the US is now expected to turn in fairly robust growth, with domestic demand
underpinned by strong private sector balance sheets and a resilient labour market. The upturn
in the global electronics cycle is showing signs of broadening beyond semiconductors and
will sustain growth in EM Asia. Although China’s growth will ease for the year as a whole amid
headwinds induced by the property sector, accommodative macropolicy settings should

sector, accommodative macropolicy settings should impart a positive impulse to the economy overall. All in, the global economy is expected to
expand by 3.0% in 2024, slightly lower than 3.3% last year.
Singapore’s GDP growth came in almost flat in Q1 2024 on a quarter-on-quarter
seasonally-adjusted basis. Activity in the manufacturing sector experienced some correction
following the electronics-led surge in the previous quarter. This drag was offset in part by a

revious quarter. This drag was offset in part by a strong boost to the tourism-related and consumer-facing industries from a few large-scale
concert events.
Growth momentum is projected to pick up over the rest of this year, with the pace of
expansion becoming more even across sectors. The manufacturing sector should expand
steadily alongside the global tech and trade upturn, with attendant positive spillovers to the
trade-related services sectors. The expected easing in global interest rates in the later part of
sing in global interest rates in the later part of the year should also bolster a range of financial services activities. Meanwhile, growth in the

cial services activities. Meanwhile, growth in the Macroeconomic Policy 67

undergirded by solid construction activity and still-recovering tourist inflows. In aggregate,
growth across the major sectors of the economy is expected to gradually converge to pre-
pandemic norms.
For the full year, Singapore’s GDP growth should strengthen to around its potential rate
and come in between 1–3%. Consequently, the negative output gap that had opened up in

tly, the negative output gap that had opened up in 2023 is forecast to narrow and close by the end of the year (Chart 4.1).

Inflation will remain on a gradually moderating path over the rest of the year
MAS Core Inflation came in slightly lower than expected in year-on-year terms in Q1 2024
and was unchanged from the previous quarter. Inflation in essential services and goods rose

er. Inflation in essential services and goods rose as earlier increases in costs passed through to prices, while the step-up in the GST rate to 9%
also kept inflation elevated for a broad swathe of CPI components. However, inflation for
travel-related services, such as hotel accommodation and airfares, eased unexpectedly
alongside favourable external supply conditions. Food inflation, including for non-cooked
food as well as discretionary services such as restaurant meals, also moderated in line with

h as restaurant meals, also moderated in line with ebbing business cost pressures. A range of alternative inflation indicators affirm that
underlying inflation in the economy continues to gradually moderate.
At the same time, private transport and accommodation inflation fell, as car prices and
residential property rents rose at a slower pace due to a greater supply of COE quotas and
completed housing units, respectively. Consequently, CPI-All Items inflation declined
discernibly in Q1.

PI-All Items inflation declined
discernibly in Q1. In line with MAS’ prior expectations, the pace of the decline in core inflation is slowing.
A smaller drag from falling imported prices, as well as the ongoing catch up of some services
prices to earlier cost increases, has kept inflation from moderating as quickly as in 2023. In
particular, inflation has been increasingly driven by labour-intensive consumer-facing services
that tend to adjust their fees less frequently. These include, for instance, education and

Nevertheless, inflation should continue to gradually moderate over the rest of 2024 amid
broadly subdued imported price pressures and easing unit labour cost growth. Although
crude oil prices have risen in recent months, their near-term inflationary impact is expected

s, their near-term inflationary impact is expected to be contained for now, and will be offset by easing inflation for other imported goods and
services. Global prices of most food commodities as well as intermediate and final goods
have remained stable or declined. In addition, as demand-supply conditions in the global
travel industry continue to rebalance, the rate of price increases for travel-related services is
expected to slow. These global price trends should be further filtered by the continuing

rends should be further filtered by the continuing appreciation of the S$NEER. As such, barring further shocks, a broad range of import prices
are projected to exert a drag on core inflation for a second consecutive year in 2024.
Meanwhile, resident wage growth eased in Q4 2023 and is expected to remain moderate
alongside cooling labour demand over most of this year. Labour productivity growth is also
anticipated to improve, as an investment pickup leads to a revival in capital deepening.

nt pickup leads to a revival in capital deepening. Consequently, unit labour costs will increase at a significantly slower pace compared to the
preceding two years. As the catch-up of consumer prices to permanently higher cost levels
progresses further over the course of the year, inflationary pressures across a broad range of
CPI components should also moderate more discernibly by late-2024.
All in, core inflation is forecast to stay elevated in the immediate quarters ahead before

ay elevated in the immediate quarters ahead before stepping down more discernibly in Q4. Meanwhile, easing private transport and
accommodation inflation will exert a drag on CPI-All Items inflation. For 2024 as a whole, both
core and headline inflation are projected to average 2.5–3.5%. Excluding the transitory effects
of the increases in the GST rate, headline and core inflation are expected to come in at 1.5–
2.5%. Inflation should continue to moderate into 2025, reflecting in part the fading impact of

into 2025, reflecting in part the fading impact of the GST, carbon tax, and water price increases.
The risks to the inflation outlook are tilted to the upside and will continue to evolve amid
heightened global uncertainties. Global commodity supply disruptions, including from
broadening geopolitical conflicts, could result in higher-than-expected energy prices. While
some supply shocks may be short-lived and result in commodity prices rapidly reverting, other

esult in commodity prices rapidly reverting, other shocks, such as a sharp rise in oil demand due to precautionary building of oil buffers, could
fuel more sustained increases in prices. At the same time, global growth could also pick up
by more than anticipated in H2 2024, generating external inflationary pressures while fuelling
stronger domestic economic growth. A broad-based and sustained uplift in labour demand
could lead to a re-acceleration in wage growth and persistently high inflation.

on in wage growth and persistently high inflation. Downside risks from weaker-than-expected global demand remain. In the major
economies, economic activity appears to be resilient. However, there are still lingering risks
from higher for longer global interest rates and capital flow volatility. An escalation in
geopolitical conflicts could also lead to an abrupt increase in financial market stress and
heightened uncertainty, dampening global and domestic growth prospects.
In April 2024, MAS kept the S$NEER on its current appreciating path

S kept the S$NEER on its current appreciating path In line with the baseline expectation that domestic GDP growth rises to around its
potential rate, the Singapore economy’s slightly negative output gap should close by year-
end. In the immediate quarters ahead, core inflation is projected to remain elevated at around
current levels. Nevertheless, as most external and domestic cost pressures continue to abate,

nal and domestic cost pressures continue to abate, Macroeconomic Policy 69
MAS Core Inflation should stay on its broadly moderating path and step down more
discernibly in Q4, before falling further in 2025.
Against this backdrop, MAS assessed that the prevailing rate of appreciation of the
policy band is needed to keep a restraining effect on imported inflation as well as domestic
cost pressures. Barring the materialisation of shocks that displace growth and inflation onto

of shocks that displace growth and inflation onto a fundamentally different trajectory, the current appreciation path of the S$NEER policy band
remains sufficient for ensuring medium term price stability.
MAS therefore decided to maintain the prevailing rate of appreciation of the S$NEER
policy band in April 2024. There was no change to the width of the band or the level at which
it was centred.
There are risks to the inflation and growth outlook stemming from shocks to global

and growth outlook stemming from shocks to global energy prices and trade, as well as financial stresses arising from an unexpected tightening
in global financial conditions. MAS will closely monitor global and domestic economic
developments and remains vigilant to risks to inflation and growth. Chart 4.2 summarises the
recent shifts in monetary policy, GDP growth and inflation in the Singapore economy.

4.2 Fiscal Policy
Budget 2024 bolstered targeted near-term support, and set out the
Government’s longer-term priorities
Budget 2024 was presented in a context of improving economic growth prospects for
Singapore, even as inflation remained a concern. While inflation was expected to decline

a concern. While inflation was expected to decline further over the year, overall cost and price levels in the economy had risen significantly.
The prices of some essential goods and services, such as water and healthcare, would also
continue to increase in 2024.
The Budget aimed to address these cost concerns, particularly for vulnerable
households and firms, even as the overall fiscal package was appropriately sized for the stage
of the business cycle. It also set out the first instalment of policy measures based on the long-

t instalment of policy measures based on the long- term economic and social objectives distilled from the Forward Singapore exercise.
A significant announcement was the measures aimed at providing structural support for
workers throughout their careers and during periods of involuntary unemployment.
Additionally, measures were introduced to reduce income inequality, promote social mobility,
and increase investment and R&D. Overall, Budget 2024 built on previous Budget initiatives,

Budget 2024 built on previous Budget initiatives, with the aim of advancing Singapore further in productivity-led economic growth and a more
inclusive society.

Additional transfers were provided to households and firms to cope with
higher costs
Amid weak real wage growth last year, Budget 2024 provided an additional $1.9 billion
to alleviate cost-of-living pressures for households under the Assurance Package. The
support came in the form of cash transfers, vouchers, and rebates largely targeted towards
lower- and middle-income households. For instance, there were additional tranches of

s. For instance, there were additional tranches of Community Development Council (CDC) Vouchers, a Cost-of-Living Special Payment, and
additional one-off U-Save and Service and Conservancy Charges (S&CC) rebates. Budget
2024 also provided a 50% personal income tax rebate of up to $200 per tax resident individual
for Year of Assessment (YA) 2024.
For businesses, Budget 2024 introduced the $1.3 billion Enterprise Support Package to
help tide firms over the prolonged period of elevated cost pressures. The package included a

of elevated cost pressures. The package included a corporate income tax rebate, enhancements to the Enterprise Financing Scheme, and an
extension of the SkillsFuture Enterprise Credit to help mitigate costs associated with
initiatives to raise productivity. With the measures tilted towards firms that are already
transforming and restructuring, the package should help undergird the resilience of
businesses in the near term.
In addition, the Budget sought to cushion the impact of higher business costs brought

ushion the impact of higher business costs brought about by other policies that are needed to uplift wages and improve the retirement adequacy
of workers. Specifically, the maximum co-funding level under the Progressive Wage Credit
Scheme (PWCS) in 2024 was raised from 30% to 50% to help defray a larger share of
employers’ costs arising from wage increases for lower-wage workers. In light of the
forthcoming increase in the CPF contribution rate for older workers, the Budget also extended

n rate for older workers, the Budget also extended the CPF Transition Offset for another year to cover half of the increase in employer
contributions. These measures should ensure that the impact of policy moves to uplift wages
and bolster retirement adequacy are smoothed out and do not excessively burden firms at a
time when many are confronted with elevated cost pressures.
Efforts were stepped up to boost the resilience and competitiveness of the
Singapore economy…

ence and competitiveness of the
Singapore economy… In the decades ahead, the Singapore economy will need to grapple with major structural
shifts stemming from a more uncertain external environment, technological disruptions, as
well as the green transition. Budget 2024, as the first instalment of the Forward Singapore
initiatives, laid out the Government’s roadmap for navigating these challenges.
First, the Budget significantly enhanced support for mid-career workers undergoing

enhanced support for mid-career workers undergoing career transitions. The SkillsFuture Level-Up Programme provides such workers seeking to
upskill or reskill with a SkillsFuture Credit (Mid-Career) top-up of $4,000 to pursue another full-
time diploma, and a monthly training allowance when they pursue selected full-time courses.
For workers facing involuntary unemployment, Budget 2024 announced the introduction of a
temporary financial support scheme, with details to be provided later in the year. Both sets of

ils to be provided later in the year. Both sets of measures would strengthen the resilience of workers and incentivise investments in needed
skills. The financial support provided during training would also reduce the likelihood of
workers resorting to courses that do not meaningfully improve their skills or employability, as
well as jobseekers accepting jobs that are a poor match to their skills. Overall, these measures
should support resident workers in acquiring skills for in-demand jobs, and alleviate labour
ng skills for in-demand jobs, and alleviate labour shortages due to skills mismatches.

leviate labour shortages due to skills mismatches. Macroeconomic Policy 75
Second, the Budget renewed efforts to enhance the productive capacity of the economy.
Measures were introduced to boost investment in tangible and intangible capital, and
facilitate technology transfer from the more productive sectors and firms of the economy.
The Budget also set aside a significant amount to be deployed to upgrade the nationwide
broadband network, invest in energy transition infrastructure and enhance security in clean

ition infrastructure and enhance security in clean energy, strengthen Singapore’s R&D ecosystem, and build up know-how for new technologies,
such as artificial intelligence. To attract high-value and substantive investments into
Singapore, the Budget introduced the Refundable Investment Credit, which is a tax credit with
a refundable cash feature. The Partnerships for Capability Transformation (PACT) scheme
was also enhanced to facilitate the partnerships—including in capability training,

the partnerships—including in capability training, internationalisation, and corporate venturing—between larger, more productive companies
and SMEs. The Budget also bolstered initiatives to help firms increase their energy efficiency
and adopt green solutions.
…while ensuring that economic growth remained inclusive
Budget 2024 continued to build on the Government’s push in recent years to raise the
wages of lower-income workers. Increases to the qualifying income thresholds for the

reases to the qualifying income thresholds for the Workfare Income Supplement (WIS) and Silver Support schemes, as well as adjustments to
the Local Qualifying Salary for local workers and wage ceiling under the PWCS, will help
ensure that the support to the needy and those at the lower end of the income distribution
keeps pace with economy-wide wage increases in recent years. In addition, the WIS payouts
for lower-wage workers were also increased, to help protect the purchasing power and

ncreased, to help protect the purchasing power and retirement adequacy of this vulnerable group. The annual income threshold for claiming
dependant-related personal income tax relief was doubled, to allow more taxpayers who
provide for dependant family members to enjoy these reliefs.
The Budget also strengthened social policies to lessen early disadvantages that might
reduce social mobility later in life. For instance, under the new ITE Progression Award, top-
ups to the Post-Secondary Education Account and CPF Ordinary Account amounting to

tion Account and CPF Ordinary Account amounting to $15,000 will be made to ITE graduates aged 30 and below who go on to complete a diploma
programme. Early childhood education will also be made more affordable for more families,
through lower caps on fees charged by Government-supported preschools and the extension
of preschool subsidies to all children from lower-income families, including those with non-
working mothers. The maximum fees at Special Education schools and fee caps in Special

Special Education schools and fee caps in Special Student Care Centres will also be lowered. By making education more affordable for more
students and families, these measures should help increase opportunities for them to
improve their longer-term socioeconomic outcomes.
Budget 2024 is projected to result in a modest basic budget deficit

it of $5.4 billion (0.8% of GDP) (Table 4.1). This reflects proportional increases in both operating revenue and total expenditure.

(0.4% of GDP), compared to $6.8 billion in FY2023 (1.0% of GDP). When the capitalisation and
depreciation of nationally significant infrastructure, and related interest costs and loan

nfrastructure, and related interest costs and loan expenses are included, Singapore should record a marginal surplus in its overall fiscal
position, amounting to $0.8 billion (0.1% of GDP).

The fiscal impulse (FI)1 is estimated to be modestly negative, at −0.5% of GDP in

and consistent with the improving growth prospects for the economy, even as support
continued to be targeted at those facing cost-of-living challenges.

out-of-pocket expenses are estimated to raise real aggregate disposable incomes, by about

raise real aggregate disposable incomes, by about 0.8% this year, and partially offset the drag on consumption from higher prices.

Special Feature A
Text Analysis of MAS Monetary Policy
Statements
This Special Feature presents a quantitative text analysis of the Monetary
Policy Statements issued by the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) over
the past two decades. The study focuses on three key dimensions:
sentiments, readability, and focus areas. Results may be used to assess the
effectiveness of MAS’ monetary policy communication and the public's
comprehension of the statements.

and the public's
comprehension of the statements. 1 Introduction
Effective communication is important to central banks. It facilitates public and financial
market understanding of the rationale for monetary policy decisions and helps anchor views
of the state of the economy. Like other central banks, MAS has enhanced and increased the
frequency and channels of communication over time, including through media and industry
engagements, and various publications.
Central bank communication should be easy to understand, objective, and its content

be easy to understand, objective, and its content should be relevant to the bank’s mission. As detailed in a European Central Bank Strategy
Review working paper (Assenmacher et al., 2021), effective communication builds public
trust, which in turn allows the shaping of expectations and subsequently the smooth conduct
of monetary policy. Managing expectations well can be impactful to the extent that
communication itself becomes a key intermediate instrument of policy. Central banks

y intermediate instrument of policy. Central banks including the US Federal Reserve have utilised communication regarding both current and
forward-looking actions as a key tool in their monetary policy toolkit (Kliesen et al., 2018).
To evaluate MAS’ monetary policy communication efforts, this paper examines the
Monetary Policy Statements issued by MAS over the period 2001–23, by applying quantitative
text analysis. The dimensions covered by this study are sentiments, readability, and focus

this study are sentiments, readability, and focus areas. Correlation with economic data is also studied to buttress the insights of the analysis.
2 Data and Approach
Several major central banks have evaluated the effectiveness of their monetary policy
communication. Notable examples include a study by the Reserve Bank of New Zealand
(Cherry and Tong, 2023), which found high levels of objectivity in its Monetary Policy
Statements, and a topic analysis by the European Central Bank (Fraccaroli and Giovannini,

European Central Bank (Fraccaroli and Giovannini, 2020) to discern the focus areas of its monetary policy parliamentary hearings over time.
The approach taken in this analysis follows Cherry and Tong (2023), who studied the
three primary areas of sentiments, readability and topical areas using general Natural
Language Processing (NLP) concepts including bag-of-words and cosine-similarity. These

ncluding bag-of-words and cosine-similarity. These Special Features 79
approaches are readily implementable in Python frameworks such as those developed by
Řehůřek and Sojka (2010), DeRobertis (2020) and Honnibal et al. (2020).
The analysis covers 53 MAS Monetary Policy Statements from February 2001 to October
2023.1 The dataset is first pre-processed2 before text analysis of the words is applied in four
ways as elaborated below.
Gauge of Sentiments
The sentiments conveyed by the text in the Monetary Policy Statements are assessed

ext in the Monetary Policy Statements are assessed using dictionaries that set out words associated with positive or negative sentiments. Two
authoritative dictionaries in the NLP field are used, namely the Harvard-IV dictionary geared
towards general language usage and the Loughran-McDonald dictionary intended for
economic and financial analysis. The Harvard-IV dictionary comprises 1,188 known root
words with positive connotations and 1,634 known root words with negative connotations.

1,634 known root words with negative connotations. The Loughran-McDonald dictionary consists of 140 root words with positive connotations
and 893 root words with negative connotations. Some examples of positive and negative
words from the two dictionaries are given in Table 1.3
The number of words that are associated with positive or negative sentiments in each
dictionary is derived and used to generate indicators for the direction and level of sentiments
of the statements.

Determination of Readability
The ease of readability of the Monetary Policy Statements is assessed via the use of two

y Policy Statements is assessed via the use of two indicators, the Flesch-Kincaid score and the Gunning-Fog score, which use proxies to capture
the complexity of the statements and education attainment required to understand them.
1 The Monetary Policy Statements analysed include the off-cycle monetary policy announcements, for example in 2001, 2015
and 2022.
2 All words are first converted to lowercase letters, with punctuation and stop words that do not contribute meaningfully to

stop words that do not contribute meaningfully to the analysis such as "and” and “or" removed. Words are then converted to their original form (e.g., “car’s” and “cars” to
“car”) in a process known as lemmatisation, which is the grouping together of words of different forms or inflections but
of the same meaning.
3 The roots or stems of the words instead of the complete words are given in the dictionaries to capture different
expressions of the words. For example, “improv” is included instead of “improve”, so that “improving” would be captured.

“improve”, so that “improving” would be captured. 80 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
Alignment with Industry Sentiments
The correlation between sentiments conveyed by the emotive words in the Monetary
Policy Statements and by industry indicators are computed to compare the degree to which
these sentiments vary together. The presence of a strong correlation between these
sentiments is taken as evidence of effective communication between policymakers and the

fective communication between policymakers and the public. Business outlook indices, based on the Economic Development Board’s (EDB) Survey
of Business Expectations of the Manufacturing Sector and the Department of Statistics’
(DOS) Business Expectations Survey for the services sector, are used as measures of industry
sentiments in the computation of the correlation coefficients.
Topic Analysis
Finally, topic analysis is conducted based on the correspondence of terms used in the

d based on the correspondence of terms used in the Monetary Policy Statements to bags-of-words that are associated with pre-defined topics.
These bags-of-words are based on text analyses of parliamentary hearings of the European
Central Bank, Bank of England and Federal Reserve and were verified by Cherry and Tong
(2023).

0.5 is a positive or optimistic slant. There is no “right” or “wrong” slant, as it depends on the context and purpose of the texts.

dictionary (Chart 1). In comparison, the slant of the general language in the statements

The statements are found to be objective, with less than 4% of each statement
containing emotive words on average. Where emotive words are present, a strong correlation
is observed between sentiments expressed about Singapore’s monetary policy and actual
industry expectations. In terms of ease of understanding, the statements have improved in
readability over the years such that it can be followed by young adults aged 18–20 years. This

be followed by young adults aged 18–20 years. This is despite the increase in word count in the statements in the last three decades. Based on
the strong alignment with business sentiments, objectivity, and improved readability, the
statements can be seen as effective in providing guidance and communicating expectations
on the economy. In relation to focus areas, prices and inflation are the dominant themes.
While an attempt has been made to cover the relevant metrics involved in text analysis,

er the relevant metrics involved in text analysis, there are limitations to this study. Notably, the scope and breadth of the vocabulary used in
topic modelling could be expanded to capture a wider variety of expressions. In addition, the
metrics considered are not exhaustive and there could be other higher-frequency indicators
that correlate with the statements’ sentiments. Nonetheless, a preliminary investigation
conducted separately using Large Language Models for topic extraction strongly
corroborated the results of this study.

Special Feature B
Public Investment as a Stimulus
Policy
Jordan Roulleau-Pasdeloup1
This Special Feature reviews the recent literature on public investment and
highlights several findings about its effectiveness as a countercyclical policy
tool, from theoretical and empirical perspectives. First, the size of the public
investment multiplier has been shown to be larger at the effective lower bound
of monetary policy than under regular economic conditions. Second, in a low

nder regular economic conditions. Second, in a low interest rate environment, public investment should be more effective than
general government spending as a stimulus tool, as it boosts output via both
private demand and aggregate supply channels. Third and finally, in the
context of a small open economy like Singapore, the effects of public
investment on output depend strongly on the reaction of the real exchange
rate to the initial stimulus.
1 Introduction
In the national accounts of developed economies, government spending on goods and

eloped economies, government spending on goods and services is usually broken down into two categories: government consumption and public
investment. The first category includes public servants’ salaries and day-to-day operating
expenditures. The second usually consists of spending on public infrastructure. In the context
of Singapore, examples include the development of new MRT lines, water processing
capabilities and seaport infrastructure. In addition, the government has recently announced

In addition, the government has recently announced a plan to issue public debt in the form of Singapore Government Securities to finance public
investment in infrastructure, including coastal protection initiatives for climate resilience.
One can think of government consumption as having shorter-term effects than public
investment, which generates benefits in the medium to long run. Yet, despite its focus on the
long run, public investment has historically been used to stimulate an economy in the short

lly been used to stimulate an economy in the short run. This idea to use government spending as short-term stimulus dates back to John
Maynard Keynes. Indeed, in Keynes’ General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money, he
famously pushes forward the idea to use government spending as an alternative tool to
combat a recession. Back then, this was quite controversial: the consensus was that a
recession cannot last forever as prices will fall enough to induce an aggregate demand

ces will fall enough to induce an aggregate demand rebound. This policy of laissez-faire was followed during the early stages of the Great
Depression, with a dismal track record: up to 25% of the working-age population ended up
unemployed. In this context, Franklin D. Roosevelt’s creation of the Public Works
Administration marked a turning point, as it led to one of the strongest GDP expansions
recorded in US history. Notably, Roosevelt’s stimulus consisted mostly of public investment
in infrastructure (e.g., roads, dams, bridges).

nt
in infrastructure (e.g., roads, dams, bridges). 1 Jordan Roulleau-Pasdeloup is an Assistant Professor of Economics at the National University of Singapore. The views
expressed herein are the author’s and should not be attributed to the Monetary Authority of Singapore or the National
University of Singapore.

Singapore or the National
University of Singapore. 90 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
In this Special Feature, we will review what the economics literature has to say about the
effects of public investment as a stimulus tool. The two most recent surges in public
investment in developed economies occurred in the context of the Great Recession and the
COVID-19 pandemic. In both cases, public investment was increased against a low-interest
rate environment. Perhaps more relevant is the fact that these low interest rates were not

is the fact that these low interest rates were not expected to react to the increase in public investment. This perspective is interesting for
Singapore as well, as short-term interest rates in Singapore closely track global interest rates,
and as such, one would not expect market interest rates to react after an increase in public
investment in Singapore.
We will first see how public investment affects the economy in theory using a standard
New Keynesian model as our guiding framework. More specifically, we will describe how the

ework. More specifically, we will describe how the effects of public investment are expected to be different in a low versus normal interest rate
environment. We will then move on to what the empirical literature has to say about the
effects of public investment. Finally, we will point out that in a small open economy, the focus
on fixed interest rates only goes so far: one has to consider the reaction of real exchange
rates as well.
2 Public Investment: Theory
In the analysis of the impact of public investment on aggregate macroeconomic

ct of public investment on aggregate macroeconomic variables, greater progress has been made on the theoretical than empirical front, following
the observation made by Prescott’s (1986) celebrated paper. The seminal reference on the
topic is Baxter and King (1993), who studied the theoretical effects of public investment in a
standard Real Business Cycle model. They found that public investment has a sizeable
multiplier effect, which was later put into perspective in Leeper et al. (2009) who showed that

erspective in Leeper et al. (2009) who showed that the necessary tax adjustments would greatly dampen the long-run effects of better public
infrastructure. In that paper, they assume that government investment is financed by a mix of
debt, transfers and various distortionary taxes. The common point of these two contributions
is that they work with a model that has no role for monetary policy or (endogenous) aggregate
demand developments. In the aftermath of the Great Recession and the COVID-19 recession

of the Great Recession and the COVID-19 recession where public investment stimulus policies occurred at the zero lower bound (ZLB) of
monetary policy, it has become more difficult to argue that these features can be overlooked.
We set out to fix this shortcoming in Bouakez et al. (2017). In that paper, we instead work
with a New Keynesian model that features a non-trivial role for both aggregate demand and
monetary policy. The government finances its spending via lump-sum taxes. The key result

es its spending via lump-sum taxes. The key result from the paper is that the stance of monetary policy matters a great deal for the size of the
public investment multiplier. In fact, we find that if a government tries to stimulate economic
activity through public investment in normal times, the effects would be rather disappointing:
an additional dollar of public investment would yield strictly less than that in terms of
increased GDP. To understand why, we need to dig a bit deeper into the mechanisms
associated with public investment.

the mechanisms
associated with public investment. In a typical New Keynesian model where firms have some degree of market power,
higher public investment generally increases aggregate demand. To be more precise, let me
define aggregate demand as the sum of private demand (consumption plus investment) and
government demand. The government needs to buy goods (say concrete, machinery, etc.)
and pay workers to build a new road. This generates higher incomes for suppliers of the

This generates higher incomes for suppliers of the goods and workers enjoy a positive income boost. In turn, inflation increases through the
aggregate Phillips curve. Whether or not this increase in government demand translates into

this increase in government demand translates into Special Features 91
a more than proportional increase in aggregate demand depends on how private demand
reacts. This is where the stance of monetary policy comes in. Responding to the higher
inflation, the central bank will increase interest rates and thereby dampen the expansionary
effects of public investment through a decrease in private demand. The end result is an
underwhelming multiplier effect.
Public Investment at the Zero Lower Bound

effect.
Public Investment at the Zero Lower Bound The story is very different when central banks are stuck with zero interest rates. Indeed,
this happens because their desired interest rate is too negative. Increases in public
investment will raise inflation and thus push up their desired interest rate. However, in the
midst of a deep recession, central banks are highly unlikely to lift their interest rates from
zero. As such, the central bank interest rate can be considered as fixed. In this situation, the

can be considered as fixed. In this situation, the increase in public demand and inflation that we explained in the preceding paragraph will
work to increase private demand as well: aggregate demand increases more than
proportionally. Under fixed interest rates, some inflation will reduce the real interest rate. This
will push consumers to consume even more given that the real returns on saving have
decreased. In turn, firms will increase their prices, which further reduces the real interest rate.

ces, which further reduces the real interest rate. The effects of public investment stimulus at the ZLB are illustrated in Chart 1, where we
plot both the aggregate supply and private demand lines. Notice that in contrast to a typical
supply/demand diagram, the private demand line is upward-sloping. This follows from the
fact that an increase in inflation under a fixed nominal interest rate (at the ZLB) reduces the
real interest rate, which incentivises households to consume more. As a result, higher inflation

lds to consume more. As a result, higher inflation is associated with more consumption in this specific context. Note that the private demand
curve would normally slope downward in a context where the central bank is able to adjust
its interest rate.
