Supply and Demand Effects of Public Investment
In economics, we like to categorise macroeconomic policies into two buckets,
differentiated by whether they operate via demand or supply channels. Public investment
policies act on both. The private demand channel of public investment comes from higher
incomes for workers and suppliers of goods, who then increase their consumption spending.

ods, who then increase their consumption spending. The increase in aggregate supply comes later, such as when a road is eventually ready for
use. In the economics literature, we usually say that there is a “time-to-build delay”. As a result,
the effects of the delayed aggregate supply expansion are largely anticipated: households
and firms in this model anticipate that firms will be more productive and thus the economy
will feature stronger growth in future. In addition, households will come to expect higher

In addition, households will come to expect higher incomes in the future. Going back to the short run, the anticipated increase in future incomes
adds to the initial private demand boost from government spending.
The fact that public investment has a productive use means that it does not simply
operate via the shift in the New Keynesian model’s aggregate supply curve along the private
demand curve (moving from point A to B in Chart 1). The anticipated income effect manifests

Chart 1). The anticipated income effect manifests itself through a further upward shift of the private demand line: for a given inflation rate,
households want to consume more. As a result of this shift, the economy moves from point
B to point C. Given that both private and public demand increase, aggregate demand
necessarily increases more than one for one. Moreover, one can see that, at the ZLB, public
investment has a much higher multiplier compared to a typical Keynesian stimulus.

ltiplier compared to a typical Keynesian stimulus. This analysis is based on a very simple model and invites a few questions: would the
results hold in a model with private capital? After all, the main counter-argument relative to
the effectiveness of public investment is that it potentially crowds out private capital (Eden
and Kraay, 2014). Do we find a big multiplier for public investment in the data?
In Bouakez et al. (2017), we extend our analysis to a medium-scale model that has

tend our analysis to a medium-scale model that has relevant features such as unemployment and private investment, among others. In that case,
private demand also includes private investment, which might fall after an increase in public
investment. We find that this is not the case, and the presence of private capital does not
dampen the effects of public investment on GDP when the interest rate is stuck at zero. In
fact, the opposite happens: we find that private investment increases after an expansion in

private investment increases after an expansion in public investment. In a follow-up project (Bouakez et al., 2020), we studied the optimal path
of government consumption/investment following a large negative aggregate demand shock.
We found that if monetary policy is limited by the ZLB, then raising public investment relative
to government consumption is the optimal policy.
We now turn to a review of what the empirical literature on public investment has to say
about this.

rature on public investment has to say
about this. Special Features 93
3 Public Investment: Empirics
Given that exogenous variation in public investment is hard to come by, there is
somewhat limited empirical literature on the effects of public investment. Nonetheless, the
literature points to evidence that is largely in line with what the models reviewed predict.
Public investment seems to have limited effects in normal times and a much larger impact
when there is a deep recession with a binding lower bound (Boehm, 2020).

ecession with a binding lower bound (Boehm, 2020). What about the mechanisms at play? Bachmann and Sims (2012) delve deeper into the
question and point to a central mechanism: confidence. Consistent with the models and the
literature already cited, they find a limited impact of government spending shocks in normal
times. However, they do find a larger impact in recessions. In addition, they find that
government spending shocks in recessions predict productivity improvements through a

ssions predict productivity improvements through a persistent increase in the ratio of (productive) public investment relative to (unproductive)
government consumption.
The picture that emerges is then the following: government spending is usually tilted
towards productive public investment in recessions. This change in composition is usually
accompanied by a relative increase in measured confidence. The result is a government
spending multiplier that is higher in recessions. Like the models reviewed before, households

sions. Like the models reviewed before, households and firms seem to react by spending more based on their improved earnings expectations.
Most of the evidence for the effects of public investment is based on the US which, to a first
approximation, can be considered a closed economy (i.e., most of its GDP is composed of
domestic investment/consumption). To tie the story back to Singapore, let us finally review
what the economics literature has to say about the effects of government spending in a small
open economy.

ts of government spending in a small
open economy. 4 Public Investment in a Small Open Economy
The key deciding factor of the impact of public investment in a closed economy is
whether the central bank’s interest rate will react to the increase in public investment. In a
small open economy with a managed exchange rate such as Singapore, the average overnight
interest rate closely tracks global interest rates. One might then think that the results reviewed

es. One might then think that the results reviewed before apply to a small open economy, and that one should expect a sizeable multiplier. That
is only partially true in a small open economy as there is another important factor to consider:
the real exchange rate. What then does the theory and data say about the interplay between
government spending and the real exchange rate?
There are two views about the effects of government spending in a small open economy.

ts of government spending in a small open economy. According to the seminal Mundell-Fleming model, if the exchange rate is fixed, then an
increase in government spending will generate a sizeable increase in GDP. This is due to price
and wage stickiness, so that the real exchange rate does not change much. In comparison,
the classical view contends that, given more flexible nominal variables, the real exchange rate
should appreciate by a comparatively large amount. In that case, the external demand for

arge amount. In that case, the external demand for local goods decreases so that domestic GDP barely rises.
In a recent contribution, Born et al. (2023) set out to tease these two competing
narratives apart. They study a small open economy New Keynesian model in which wages are
downwardly rigid but upwardly mobile. Interestingly, they find that the impact of government
spending on GDP depends on whether fiscal policy is contractionary or expansionary. In
response to a decrease in government spending, the Mundell-Fleming view applies: after a

pending, the Mundell-Fleming view applies: after a 94 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
fall in government spending, the economy experiences a relatively large decrease in GDP as
wage rigidities limit the extent of real exchange rate depreciation. However, when fiscal policy
is expansionary, the classical view applies: an increase in government spending is mostly
absorbed by an appreciation of the real exchange rate. As a result, government spending may
not have as large a stimulative effect in a small open economy compared to a closed

ffect in a small open economy compared to a closed economy as the real exchange rate tends to appreciate and counteract the effects of
expansionary fiscal policy. The authors find that that predictions from the model were
supported by data from the European Union and a set of small open economies with fixed
exchange rates.
These results apply to government spending in general, not specifically to public
investment. In a recession, even if public investment is not expected to increase GDP much

ic investment is not expected to increase GDP much by stimulating aggregate demand directly, there is still room for the expected income effect
to play a role. In this case then, increasing public investment can be an attractive policy option
for two reasons: (1) it will be more efficient in stimulating economic activity compared to a
standard expansion in government consumption and (2) it will result in improved total factor
productivity in the medium to long run.
5 Conclusion

oductivity in the medium to long run.
5 Conclusion Is public investment an attractive policy option? As usual, the answer from the
economics literature is that it all depends on what the objective is. If the objective is to
stimulate an economy in the short run, then the theory and empirics say that this works only
in a large recession for a closed economy. For small open economies, where one would not
expect the interest rate to react, the increase in public investment could be almost completely

se in public investment could be almost completely absorbed by an appreciation of the real exchange rate. If the objective is to promote total
factor productivity growth in the medium to long run, then public investment is an attractive
policy option.
For both closed and small economies, however, one fact is clear: decreasing public
investment, especially when monetary policy is constrained, will have a significantly negative
impact on GDP in the short run. Further, it is also likely to dampen total factor productivity

is also likely to dampen total factor productivity growth in the medium to long run. Here, the answer is not “it depends”: decreasing public
investment in a recession is not a good option regardless of the objective.

is not a good option regardless of the objective. Special Features 95

Special Feature C
The Big Shift in Working
Arrangements: Eight Ways Unusual
Steven J. Davis1
The COVID-19 pandemic instigated a big shift in working arrangements. I first
describe the scale of this shift in the US, drawing on the Survey of Working
Arrangements and Attitudes and other sources.2 I then review differences,
circa 2023, in work from home rates across industries, demographic groups,

home rates across industries, demographic groups, and countries. The big shift had surprisingly benign (or even positive) effects
on productivity, which is one reason it has endured. Compared to other shocks
that strike modern economies, the big shift is also unusual in other respects:
it relaxes time-budget and locational constraints, improves flexibility in time
use, enhances individual autonomy, drives a major re-sorting of workers to
jobs and employers, and alters the structure of wages. The big shift also

alters the structure of wages. The big shift also reduces wage-growth pressures during the transition to new working
arrangements and lifestyles. The shift benefits workers, on average, even as
it lowers non-labour costs and real product wages for firms.
1 Introduction
The COVID-19 pandemic instigated a major shift in how many of us work and live. Before
the pandemic, nearly all employees travelled to their employer’s or client’s worksite on all or
almost all workdays. That’s no longer the case. Many of us now put in paid full workdays from

ase. Many of us now put in paid full workdays from home (or another remote location) one or more days per week. Some of us now work from
home most or all of the time. This big shift matters for worker well-being, wages, productivity,
managerial practices, workplace cultures, transit systems, property values, cities, and more.
In this essay, I first present evidence on the scale of the shift to work from home (WFH) in the
US. I then review evidence on differences, circa 2023, in the extent of WFH across industries,

irca 2023, in the extent of WFH across industries, demographic groups, and countries. The evidence also shows that people differ greatly in
their preferences over working arrangements.
Policymakers, investors, business executives, and researchers are accustomed to
thinking about economic shocks associated with financial crises, monetary policy, tax policy,
government spending, trade policy, commodity supplies, and geopolitical developments.
Compared to these shocks, the recent shift in working arrangements is unusual in several

hift in working arrangements is unusual in several noteworthy respects. For starters, it had surprisingly benign, even positive, effects on

ureau of Economic Research, Senior Fellow with the Asian Bureau of Finance and Economics Research, and Advisor to the Economic Policy Group at the Monetary Authority of
Singapore. The views expressed herein are the author’s and should not be attributed to the Monetary Authority of Singapore
or other institutions associated with the author.
2 This Special Feature draws heavily on my data collection efforts and my research with many collaborators, especially Jose

research with many collaborators, especially Jose Maria Barrero and Nick Bloom. Our data collection activities include the US Survey of Working Arrangements and Attitudes
and the Global Survey of Working Arrangements. The latter is a joint undertaking with Cevat Giray Aksoy, Matthias Dolls
and Pablo Zarate. Errors and omissions are my own.

and Pablo Zarate. Errors and omissions are my own. Special Features 97
productivity in many tasks and jobs. The surprise in question here is relative to the pre-
pandemic expectations of workers and managers. That WFH proved to be more practical and
effective than anticipated in many jobs and tasks helps explain why the big shift has endured.
The recent shift in working arrangements is also unusual in other ways: it relaxes time-
budget and locational constraints, improves flexibility in time use, enhances individual

roves flexibility in time use, enhances individual autonomy, drives a major re-sorting of workers to jobs and employers, and alters the structure
of wages. The big shift also reduces wage-growth pressures over the course of the transition
to a new set of arrangements in how we work and live. The shift benefits workers, on average,
even as it lowers non-labour costs and real product wages for firms.
These and other aspects of the big shift are not unprecedented. For example, the

big shift are not unprecedented. For example, the transition from an agrarian to an industrial economy involved an enormous re-sorting of
workers to jobs and profound changes in how people worked and lived. High-speed transit
systems relax locational constraints and offer at least the potential to save on commuting
time. But the industrial revolution and the later transition to a service-oriented economy
unfolded over many decades. The development of rapid transit systems also unfolded over

opment of rapid transit systems also unfolded over long periods of time and happened at different times in different places. What distinguishes
the recent shift in working arrangements is its combination of scale, abruptness, surprise
impact, and other unusual features.
2 The Big Shift in Working Arrangements
The US Experience
Consider the evolution of WFH in the US: the share of paid full workdays performed at
home drifted upward in the half-century before the COVID-19 pandemic, reaching about 7% in

before the COVID-19 pandemic, reaching about 7% in 2019 (Barrero et al., 2023). Chart 1 picks up the story from there. The WFH rate shot above
50% in spring 2020, driven by contagion fears and government restrictions on commercial
and social activities. The WFH rate fell over the following two years, stabilising at around 28%
of paid full workdays according to the US Survey of Working Arrangements and Attitudes
(SWAA) and the US Census Bureau’s Household Pulse Suvey (HPS).3,4 Evidence on working

ousehold Pulse Suvey (HPS).3,4 Evidence on working arrangements derived from the text in job vacancy postings also suggests that WFH rates in
the US have stabilised since 2022.5

There are also striking differences in WFH rates across employers in the same industry.
To develop this point, Hansen et al. (2023) exploit granular data on millions of job vacancy
postings. In one of their examples, they consider US automobile manufacturers as they recruit

nsider US automobile manufacturers as they recruit for engineers. In 2022, restricting attention to engineering positions, the share of vacancy
postings that say the job allows some remote work was zero for Tesla, 8% for Ford, 23% for
General Motors, and 45% for Honda. It was near zero for all four companies in 2019. These
observations illustrate that WFH intensity is, in part, an outcome of choices about job design,
managerial practices, and workplace culture. Organisations can make different choices in

lture. Organisations can make different choices in this regard, and they do so in the post-pandemic economy.
Differences Across Demographic Groups
WFH rates rise steeply with individual-level educational attainment in the post-pandemic
economy, as many studies document. This phenomenon overlaps with the pattern of WFH
rates across industries reported in Chart 3. In particular, highly educated workers are
concentrated in the Information, Finance & Insurance, and Professional & Business Services

& Insurance, and Professional & Business Services sectors. These sectors—and the people who work in them—have high WFH propensities for
the reasons discussed above. As one might anticipate from these remarks, WFH rates also
rise steeply with earnings (Barrero et al., 2021).
The presence of young children in the household is also robustly associated with more
WFH. That’s true for both men and women, and this pattern holds across the vast majority of
the advanced and middle-income economies considered in Aksoy et al. (2022). Survey

conomies considered in Aksoy et al. (2022). Survey evidence also reveals stronger desires to work from home part of the week by persons who
live with young children. These preference differences tied to the presence of children in the
household help explain another prominent demographic pattern: WFH rates peak among
people in their thirties and early forties, which is when parents are typically raising young
children. Women tend to work from home more than men, even conditional on the presence

me more than men, even conditional on the presence of young children, but the difference is modest in the US. The already-small average difference
in WFH rates between American men and women shrinks further or disappears altogether
after controlling for women’s greater educational attainment (Barrero et al., 2023).

Heterogeneity in Preferences Over Working Arrangements
As discussed above, preferences over working arrangements and observed WFH rates
differ systematically with education, earnings, age, sex, and parental status. Not surprisingly,

, age, sex, and parental status. Not surprisingly, people who face longer commutes also place higher value on the opportunity to WFH,
conditional on other observables. Another striking feature of the data is even more
noteworthy: people differ enormously in their preferences over working arrangements.
When asked directly about their willingness to pay for the opportunity to work from home
two or three days a week, nearly a tenth of American workers dislike the idea and require a

of American workers dislike the idea and require a pay premium to willingly do so. More than a quarter of Americans are equally happy to work
entirely at their employer’s site or to split the workweek between home and employer
premises. The rest, a majority, prefer to work from home part of the week, with wide variation
in their willingness to accept lower pay to do so. The average willingness to pay for the option
to work from home two or three days a week is 8% of one’s pay among American workers,

a week is 8% of one’s pay among American workers, according to the SWAA data considered by Barrero et al. (2021).
Aksoy et al. (2022) investigate preferences over working arrangements using G-SWA
data for 27 countries. They also find great heterogeneity in preferred working arrangements.
To highlight this heterogeneity, they regress individual-level willingness to pay for the
opportunity to work from home two or three days a week on educational attainment, age, sex,

e days a week on educational attainment, age, sex, marital status, presence of children, commute time, current working arrangements, survey
wave, and country. Even their most expansive and flexible regression specifications yield R-
squared values of less than 12%. This finding underscores the heterogeneity in preferences
over working arrangements. In plain language, people differ greatly in their preferred working
arrangements and in their willingness to trade off other considerations, like pay, to attain their

preferred arrangements. The upshot of this preference heterogeneity is that variety in working arrangements is
beneficial for society. Even if the median worker were indifferent between traditional and
hybrid working arrangements, for example, the newfound ability of individuals to sort into their
preferred arrangements is highly valuable. In fact, the evidence says that most people prefer
to work from home at least one day a week. That makes it even more socially beneficial to

3 Eight Ways Unusual
Work from Home Worked Better than Expected
The COVID-19 pandemic triggered a mass social experiment in working arrangements.
The experimentation lasted for weeks and months, sometimes longer, and extended across
most sectors of the economy. All of that experimentation revealed new information about the

experimentation revealed new information about the practicality and effectiveness of remote work across a vast array of tasks, jobs, organisations,
and commercial networks. In turn, the new information prompted many workers and
employers to reassess their working arrangements.
Barrero et al. (2021) find that most Americans were favourably surprised by their ability
to be productive in WFH mode during the pandemic. This is not to say they felt or were equally

emic. This is not to say they felt or were equally 104 Macroeconomic Review | April 2024
productive in WFH and on-site work modes. Rather, the point is that their experiences during
the pandemic gave many workers a reason to reconsider their working arrangements (and
lifestyles). The reader might doubt the accuracy of productivity self-assessments by workers.
Mindful of this concern, Barrero et al. also investigate the relationship between worker self-
assessments and employer plans for working arrangements. Looking across individual

or working arrangements. Looking across individual workers, they find that the number of WFH days that the employer plans for the employee
after the pandemic rises strongly with the employee’s assessment of WFH productivity during
the pandemic. This finding says that, on average, employee self-assessments of
effectiveness in WFH mode during the pandemic align well with the perceptions of their
employers. Aksoy et al. (2022) find the same patterns in G-SWA data for 27 countries.

the same patterns in G-SWA data for 27 countries. Some observers express puzzlement at the durability of the big shift in working
arrangements. If WFH is so great, the argument goes, why was it rare before the pandemic.
Among other weaknesses, this argument neglects the costs and consequences of
experimentation. When experimentation is sufficiently costly, it may be optimal to exploit
known production technologies rather than incur costs and run risks to try unknown and

than incur costs and run risks to try unknown and unproven technologies. This (rational) reluctance to experiment can inhibit the discovery of
more effective production technologies and working arrangements. If new circumstances
then compel experimentation or make it more attractive, the resulting flow of new information
can lead to revised assessments and new arrangements.11
Larcom et al. (2017) present evidence that a strike by London Underground workers led

ce that a strike by London Underground workers led many commuters to try new travel routes and, in some cases, to stick with the new routes
after the strike ended. The route-altering responses to the strike were larger for people who
lived in areas where the abstract depictions of the London Underground network presented a
more distorted impression of travel distances and times. In short, forced experimentation
yielded new information for commuters, causing some of them to re-optimise their choice of
travel routes.

them to re-optimise their choice of
travel routes. The COVID-19 pandemic triggered a forced experimentation in working arrangements,
and it did so at scale in countries around the world. The simultaneity of experimentation
across suppliers, producers, customers, and commercial networks yielded information and
experience that were hard or impossible to acquire before the pandemic. In this respect, the
pandemic-instigated experimentation with working arrangements differs in kind from the

with working arrangements differs in kind from the experimentation in travel routes triggered by the London Underground strike. It was entirely
feasible for individual commuters to try alternative travel routes before the strike. In contrast,
before the pandemic it was impossible for a large professional services firm, say, to
experiment with fully remote work while all of its clients also operated in a fully remote mode.
For this reason, the social distancing and lockdown restrictions associated with the pandemic

lockdown restrictions associated with the pandemic revealed information about the practicality and effectiveness of working arrangements that
no worker or firm could have confidently acquired before the pandemic.
Obviously, the pandemic-instigated experimentation was quite costly and highly
disruptive. By revealed preference, firms and workers experimented in ways they had
preferred to avoid before the pandemic. Nevertheless, those experiences generated new

mic. Nevertheless, those experiences generated new knowledge about the practicality and effectiveness of alternative working arrangements. For
many workers and employers, that new knowledge led to a re-optimisation and lasting shift
in the choice of working arrangements.

Other Unusual Aspects of the Big Shift
In addition to a flood of new information about the practicality and effectiveness of
alternative working arrangements, the pandemic-instigated big shift has many other unusual
aspects and consequences. I briefly discuss several of them in the balance of this section.
1. Time Savings
Barrero et al. (2021) quantify the time savings associated with the big shift to WFH in

me savings associated with the big shift to WFH in the US. When American employees work from home, they save an average of 65 minutes per
day. The time savings arise mainly from avoided commutes, but Americans also devote about
ten minutes less to grooming and dressing on WFH days as compared to on-site workdays.
Aggregating and accounting for individual-level working arrangements before and after the
pandemic, Barrero et al. (2021) estimate that the total time savings associated with the big

hat the total time savings associated with the big shift equals about 2% of pre-pandemic work hours on an earnings-weighted basis.12 This
figure is an average over people who participated in the big shift, and those who did not.
What’s the economic value of these time savings? The after-tax wage rate offers a useful
benchmark for the private value of time savings when the individual freely allocates time
across activities, as in Becker (1965), and time spent commuting is neither more nor less

and time spent commuting is neither more nor less (un)pleasant than time spent working. Using this benchmark, the time savings associated
with the big shift are worth about 2% of after-tax earnings in the US. That’s a large beneficial
consequence of the big shift. At the risk of belabouring the obvious, this figure quantifies a
flow benefit. The present value benefits of the time savings due to the big shift are twenty
times larger at a 5% annual discount rate.

twenty
times larger at a 5% annual discount rate. Aksoy et al. (2023a) carry out related calculations for workers, aged 20–59, who finished
secondary school in 27 countries. While they cannot compare pre-pandemic and post-
pandemic outcomes, they have individual-level data on working arrangements and commute
times as of 2021 and 2022. The average daily time savings when working from home is 72
minutes in their sample. When they account for the incidence of WFH across people—

ey account for the incidence of WFH across people— including those who never work remotely—they estimate that WFH saves an average of one
hour per week per worker in the post-pandemic economy. For a full-time worker, this average
time savings is 2.2% of a 46-hour workweek (40 paid hours plus six hours of commuting). Of
course, average time savings are smaller (larger) in countries with lower (higher) WFH rates
and shorter (longer) commutes.
In short, the big shift to WFH relaxes the time budget constraint for millions of workers

the time budget constraint for millions of workers around the world. WFH also reduces the money costs of commuting and often requires lower
expenditures on clothing, laundering, and workday dining.
2. Greater Flexibility in Time Use Over the Day
When asked “What are the top three benefits of working from home?” in the February
2022 SWAA, the top two choices were “No commute” (60%) and “Flexible work schedule”
(49%). This response pattern confirms that many workers place high value on the flexibility in

any workers place high value on the flexibility in time use afforded by WFH. It’s easy to see why. Working from home two days a week, for
example, makes it much easier to coordinate with plumbers or electricians for home repairs,
accompany a child to the dentist, or simply chat with your child for a few minutes when he or
she returns from school. It also makes it easier to take a work break for exercise or a stroll in

the park, while working later into the evening to compensate. In short, WFH offers greater
flexibility in time use over the workday, which is likely to be especially valuable for parents
with children in the household.
3. More Personal Autonomy
For many people, home offers a more pleasant and productive working environment than
the office. At home, the individual controls the ambient temperature and humidity, dresses as

s the ambient temperature and humidity, dresses as he or she sees fit (subject to the requirements of video conferencing), listens to background
music or not, and so on. Some people have physical infirmities that are easier to
accommodate at home. Some people find it much easier to focus at home. Some people
must contend with obnoxious or abusive coworkers in the office. Additionally, some people
hold religious beliefs or political views that clash with those of coworkers.13

itical views that clash with those of coworkers.13 My point here is not to argue that WFH is suitable for all workers, tasks, jobs, and
organisations. Clearly, it is not. Rather my point is that variety in available working
arrangements is socially valuable because it works well for some people in some jobs, and
because some people like the greater personal autonomy that comes with WFH over and
above the benefits associated with time savings and greater flexibility in time use.
4. Relaxed Locational Constraints

ity in time use.
4. Relaxed Locational Constraints Historically, the choice of where to live was tightly tethered to the location of one’s job.
The big shift to WFH relaxes that locational constraint. In doing so, it expands employment
options for anyone who can work in jobs that are suitable for hybrid or fully remote working
arrangements. It also relaxes residential location choices for individuals and families.
Consider a software engineer who lives (or wants to live) in a small town that is located

(or wants to live) in a small town that is located fifty miles away from the nearest city with software jobs. Commuting fifty miles each way five
days a week is an unappealing proposition for most people. The same commute once a week
is not so onerous. As this example suggests, the big shift in working arrangements can be
especially valuable for people who live (or want to live) outside of urban areas.
A similar logic applies to married couples that must contend with joint-location

ried couples that must contend with joint-location constraints as they seek to manage their separate careers while raising a family or simply
sharing a life together. If each member of the couple must commute to the workplace five
days a week, it may be impractical or unappealing to work for employers situated 100 miles
apart. The same employers and jobs become much more manageable if one or both members
of the couple must commute only two days a week.
The big shift also offers new opportunities for employers to recruit employees from

ortunities for employers to recruit employees from areas with deeper talent pools or lower wages, without relocating the business. Akan et al.
(2024) provide some evidence on this score by exploiting data from Gusto, a firm that
provides payroll processing and other services to mostly smaller- and mid-sized employers.
They analyse employee-level data linked to a balanced panel of 5,800 firms that operated
continuously from 2018 to 2023. As of 2019, less than 1% of the employees at these firms

2019, less than 1% of the employees at these firms resided more than 50 miles from their employer’s worksite. By 2023, about 2% of the
employees hired before March 2020 at these firms lived more than 50 miles away. Among
employees at these firms who were hired since March 2020, more than 7% live more than 50
miles away from the employer’s worksite as of 2023. This result says that a partial untethering

of worker residential locations from employer worksite locations is underway. This process
will surely continue to unfold for many years, as company workforces gradually turn over.
5. The Re-Sorting of Workers to Jobs and Employers
In light of their own individual WFH experiences in 2020, the newfound availability of
WFH options and the relaxation of locational constraints, many workers chose to quit their

onal constraints, many workers chose to quit their old jobs in favour of new ones with more appealing working arrangements. And many, but not
all, employers came to see flexible working arrangements as helpful to their talent recruitment
and retention efforts. Even among firms in the same industry competing for talent in the same
occupational category, the range of working arrangements on offer is much wider now than
before the pandemic.
These developments spurred a surge of worker quits in what is sometimes dubbed the

ge of worker quits in what is sometimes dubbed the “Great Resignation,” but which is more aptly described as the “Great Re-Sorting”. According
to statistics from the US Job Openings and Labour Turnover Survey, the quit rate among
American employees reached record highs in early 2021. Throughout the period from early
2021 to early 2023, US quit rates were higher than any previously recorded quit rate, extending
back to the start of the series in December 2020.14
Bagga et al. (2023) develop a calibrated equilibrium search model with frictional

alibrated equilibrium search model with frictional unemployment that accommodates the amenity-value aspect of WFH in some jobs. Their
model explains several unusual features of US labour market dynamics in the wake of the
COVID-19 pandemic: the unprecedented surge in quit rates, high vacancy rates, low vacancy
fill rates, the peculiar behaviour of the Beveridge Curve, the striking drop in matching
efficiency, and the Great Re-Sorting of workers to new jobs. Their model also accounts for

workers to new jobs. Their model also accounts for much of the cross-industry variation in these patterns as a consequence of industry
differences in the share of jobs that offer WFH opportunities. Their analysis reinforces my
view that the big shift in working arrangements is a highly unusual and consequential shock
in several respects. Several otherwise puzzling labour market developments in recent years
are easy to understand once we recognise the big shift in working arrangements and the
amenity-value gains that it brought.

ments and the
amenity-value gains that it brought. It is unclear to me (as of April 2024) whether and to what extent a similar dynamic in the
joint behaviour of quits, unemployment, vacancies, and vacancy fill rates has played out in
other countries since 2020, especially countries that saw shifts to WFH comparable in
magnitude to that of the US. Research on this topic would be quite useful.
6. Reduced Wage-Growth Pressures along the Transition Path
As we have seen, the big shift in working arrangements raises the amenity value of

n working arrangements raises the amenity value of employment in many jobs. These amenity-value gains take the form of time savings, greater
flexibility in time use, more personal autonomy, and more locational flexibility. Economic
reasoning implies that employers and workers ultimately share the resulting amenity-value
gains associated with the big shift. Since workers initially reaped the direct benefits of the
shift at pre-determined wages—i.e., wages set before the pandemic struck—employer

.e., wages set before the pandemic struck—employer benefits take the form of slower wage growth along the transition path to a new equilibrium
with compensation packages that recognise higher remote work levels.

Barrero et al. (2022) develop novel survey evidence to assess this mechanism and

ten executives said their firms relied on expanded WFH to moderate wage-growth pressures
when looking back 12 months from April/May 2022. A similar share of executives said their

May 2022. A similar share of executives said their firms expected to rely on WFH to moderate wage growth over the next 12 months, as of
April/May 2022. When executives said that expanded WFH opportunities moderated wage
growth at their firm, the survey asked how much. Integrating over all firm-level responses, and
weighting each firm in proportion to its employment level, Barrero et al. find that the big shift
to WFH reduced overall wage growth by about 2 percentage points over two years centred

y about 2 percentage points over two years centred around April/May 2022. This wage-growth restraint came at a fortuitous time for the Federal
Reserve, as it reacted to an inflation surge and sought to return inflation rates to acceptable
levels.
The shift to remote work affects labour costs in other ways as well. Barrero et al. (2022)
present evidence that increased reliance on remote work at the firm level is associated with
more use of independent contractors, leased employees, domestic outsourcing and foreign

leased employees, domestic outsourcing and foreign offshoring. These developments are also likely to reduce labour costs. In addition, fully
remote employees do not require office space and the overhead costs that come with a
physical footprint. To a lesser extent, hybrid working arrangements also let firms economise
on space.
It’s important to recognise that these employer cost savings do not come at the expense
of their employees. WFH yields benefits that most workers appreciate and that some value

s that most workers appreciate and that some value greatly. Moreover, the relaxation of locational constraints afforded by WFH can
simultaneously raise real worker wages and lower real product wages. To see this point,
consider an employee who accepts a 10% nominal wage cut in exchange for performing his
job remotely and re-locating to another city with living costs that are 20% lower. In this
example, the employee’s real wage rises by about 10% and the employer’s real cost of

rises by about 10% and the employer’s real cost of securing his labour services falls by 10%. Both employer and employee benefit.
7. Persistent Wage-Structure Effects
The big shift to WFH alters the structure of wages as well. To see how, recall that WFH
rates rise steeply with educational attainment in the cross section, and that WFH jobs cluster
in certain industry sectors (Chart 3). Thus, the amenity-value benefits associated with the big
shift are concentrated among these same workers and sectors. A long line of thinking in

me workers and sectors. A long line of thinking in economics says that wages are lower, other things equal, in jobs with amenity attributes that
workers like.15 Thus, it seems likely that the big shift shrank the college wage premium and
put more downward pressure on wages in sectors with larger increases in WFH intensity.
While limited, the available evidence favours this hypothesis. In their survey of business
executives, Barrero et al. (2022) find smaller wage-growth moderation effects in sectors with

ler wage-growth moderation effects in sectors with few jobs that are suitable for remote work and larger moderation effects in sectors with many
such jobs. Autor et al. (2023) document a large and “unexpected compression” in the US wage
distribution after the pandemic struck, including a reduction in the college wage premium.
Their explanation stresses the pandemic’s effects on labour market tightness and wage
markdowns, but they observe that amenity-value shocks may also play a role.

4 Concluding Remarks
The big shift in working arrangements benefits workers, on average, even as it lowers
non-labour costs and real product wages for firms. To be sure, managers and organisations
face new challenges in adjusting to hybrid and remote working arrangements. Some
observers worry that the shift to remote work will impede learning on the job and professional
networking. Some worry that a drop in face-to-face encounters in the workplace will slow the

-to-face encounters in the workplace will slow the pace of innovation. The shift to WFH and the loss of inward commuters also present huge
challenges for some cities, including Chicago (my previous home), San Francisco (close to
my current home) and Washington, D.C. (my frequent travel destination). These concerns are
serious ones, and they warrant attention.
While mindful of the costs and challenges, I see the big shift in working arrangements
as, on balance, highly beneficial for reasons reviewed in this essay and for other reasons.16

Preface
The main chapters of this Review provide a comprehensive analysis of
the conjunctural developments and outlook for the global and
domestic economies. These are integral components of MAS’
monetary policy decisions.

Key Messages
The Singapore economy faces headwinds from global trade tensions
The global economy grew steadily in 2024, underpinned by robust domestic demand in the
US and strong export performance in Asia. However, prospects for global GDP growth have
diminished amid the trade conflict. Economies that levy duties on imports will likely
experience an increase in costs, which will in turn weigh on business and consumer spending.

l in turn weigh on business and consumer spending. Meanwhile, exporting countries which have been hit by tariffs will experience a negative
external demand shock. As a whole, global GDP growth is expected to slow below trend.
Against this backdrop, Singapore’s growth outlook has turned more cautious. As a small and
open economy, Singapore will be heavily impacted by trade conflicts, with external-facing
industries such as manufacturing likely to see slower demand. The financial sector could see

see slower demand. The financial sector could see reduced credit intermediation activity from a slowdown in investment spending, while a sharp
rise in risk-off sentiment could affect fees and commissions in the sector.
Growth in the Singapore economy is expected to slow to 0.0–2.0% in 2025 from 4.4% in 2024.
As a consequence, Singapore’s output gap will turn negative this year.
Inflation has fallen significantly and is expected to be low this year
Both MAS Core and CPI-All Items inflation fell significantly in Q1, reflecting to a large extent

significantly in Q1, reflecting to a large extent the broad-based moderation in underlying price pressures. Soft domestic spending on F&B
services and retail goods as well as receding business cost pressures dampened the pace of
consumer price increases.
For the rest of 2025, domestic demand and cost pressures will be more moderate than
previously anticipated. Higher global tariffs are assessed to be disinflationary for Singapore
on net, as weaker economic activity abroad and in Singapore weighs on imported and
domestic cost increases.

re weighs on imported and
domestic cost increases. Consequently, both MAS Core Inflation and CPI-All Items inflation are now projected to come
in lower than previously expected, averaging 0.5–1.5% in 2025. This is a decline from the 2.8%
and 2.4% recorded for core and headline inflation in 2024, respectively.
In April, MAS eased monetary policy by reducing the rate of appreciation of
the S$NEER policy band
MAS aims to secure low and stable inflation over the medium term by managing the path of

ation over the medium term by managing the path of S$ nominal effective exchange rate (S$NEER) within a policy band.
In April 2025, MAS reduced the rate of appreciation of the S$NEER policy band slightly. There
was no change to the width of the policy band or the level at which it is centred. This
adjustment is consistent with a modest and gradual appreciation path of the S$NEER policy
band that will ensure medium-term price stability.

band that will ensure medium-term price stability. Key Messages 7
There is significant uncertainty over how trade policy actions will unfold in the period ahead.
Prolonged trade conflicts could pose further downside risks to growth, and thus inflation.
Conversely, trade tensions may de-escalate over time, which could boost sentiments and
export activity. MAS is closely monitoring these global and domestic economic
developments.

14 April 2025
Monetary Policy Statement
INTRODUCTION
1. In January this year, MAS kept the Singapore dollar nominal effective exchange rate (S$NEER)
policy band on a modest and gradual appreciation path, but reduced its slope slightly. There was no
change to the width of the policy band or the level at which it was centred. Since then, the S$NEER
has fluctuated within the upper half of the policy band in response to shifts in the macroeconomic

cy band in response to shifts in the macroeconomic outlook and shocks to global trade policies. Notwithstanding the volatility, the average level of the
S$NEER over the last three months has been broadly unchanged from that in the preceding three
months.
Chart 1
S$ Nominal Effective Exchange Rate (S$NEER)
GROWTH BACKDROP
2. Singapore’s key trading partners showed signs of weakening economic activity in the first
quarter of 2025. Consumer and business confidence in major economies have fallen alongside

onfidence in major economies have fallen alongside escalating trade policy uncertainty. Concomitantly, retail sales and capex intentions have softened.
In the region, export performances across economies have turned more uneven, reflecting the
waning of temporary boosts to production and shipments from trade frontloading, even as underlying
AI-driven demand for electronics saw some resilience.
3. MTI’s Advance Estimates show that the Singapore economy expanded by 3.8% year-on-year

he Singapore economy expanded by 3.8% year-on-year in Q1 2025. At the same time, the growth momentum was weaker than expected, contracting by 0.8%
on a quarter-on-quarter seasonally-adjusted basis, down from the 0.5% expansion a quarter ago. In
tandem with the nascent slowdown in some major economies, activity in the external-facing
manufacturing and modern services sectors declined. Meanwhile, the domestic-oriented sectors
saw generally tepid activity.

4 . Prospects for global trade and GDP growth dimmed in early April. The US has imposed tariffs
on imports from most countries in the world, with some of these countries announcing retaliatory
tariffs. Economies that levy duties on imports will likely experience an increase in costs and this will
weigh on their aggregate demand. At the same time, exporting countries which have been hit by

e time, exporting countries which have been hit by tariffs will be confronted with weaker demand and pressure to lower prices for their output. In
addition, global financial conditions have tightened as asset markets have begun repricing risks in
the global economy. These factors will exert widespread and potentially reinforcing drags on
production, trade, and investments in Singapore’s major trading partners. Global growth is expected
to weaken this year, with trade possibly moderating to a greater extent.

ith trade possibly moderating to a greater extent. 5. For 2025 as a whole, Singapore’s GDP growth is expected to slow to 0.0–2.0% from 4.4% last
year. Given Singapore’s high trade dependency and deep integration with global supply chains,
slowing global and regional trade as well as heightened policy uncertainty will weigh on the external-
facing sectors, which could spill over into the domestic-oriented sectors. Consequently, the
aggregate level of output will come in below the economy’s potential this year.

l come in below the economy’s potential this year. 6. The external environment remains uncertain. There are downside risks to Singapore’s
economic outlook stemming from episodes of financial market volatility and a sharper-than-
expected fall in final demand abroad. A more abrupt or persistent weakening in global trade will have
significant ramifications on Singapore’s trade-related sectors, and in turn, the broader economy.
INFLATION OUTLOOK
7. MAS Core Inflation1 eased significantly to 0.7% y-o-y in Jan–Feb 2025, from 1.9% in Q4 2024.

0.7% y-o-y in Jan–Feb 2025, from 1.9% in Q4 2024. Inflation fell by more than expected across a wide range of goods and services. Soft consumer
spending on F&B services and retail goods domestically, as well as moderating cost pressures, have
dampened consumer price increases. Enhanced government subsidies also contributed to lower
services inflation. The re-basing of the CPI in January this year accounted for only a small part of the
step down in inflation.

r only a small part of the
step down in inflation. 8. MAS Core Inflation is now forecast to average 0.5–1.5% in 2025, down from 1.0–2.0% in the
January 2025 MPS. The downgrade reflects the lower-than-expected inflation outturns in Jan–Feb
as well as an anticipated moderation in the pace of price increases amid the weakening economic
outlook. On the external front, imported inflation should be modest, as slowing global demand and
lower energy commodity prices dampen imported costs. Domestic unit labour cost increases are

ted costs. Domestic unit labour cost increases are also expected to moderate due to easing nominal wage growth and improving labour productivity.
Together with softer consumer spending, as well as enhanced government subsidies, these factors
should temper inflation in the quarters ahead.
9. CPI-All Items inflation in 2025 is similarly expected to average 0.5–1.5%, down from 1.5–2.5%
previously. This reflects in part the low outturns in Jan–Feb as well as the downgrade to the MAS
Core Inflation forecast range.

MONETARY POLICY 10. Amid the weakening external outlook, Singapore’s output gap will turn negative. Consequently,
imported and domestic cost pressures will remain low and MAS Core Inflation is forecast to stay
well below 2%. The risks to inflation are tilted towards the downside.

11. MAS will continue with the policy of a modest and gradual appreciation of the S$NEER policy
band. However, the rate of appreciation will be reduced slightly. There will be no change to the width
of the band and the level at which it is centred.
12. MAS will closely monitor global and domestic economic developments, and remain vigilant to
risks to inflation and growth.

1 Financial Conditions
1.1 Global Developments
Sentiments in global financial markets have weakened since early April
Following the US imposition of tariffs worldwide in early April, global financial market volatility
and risk aversion have risen sharply. Since 2 April, the MSCI All Country Index has fallen by
5%1, bringing losses since the beginning of the year to 6%. The prices of growth-sensitive

of the year to 6%. The prices of growth-sensitive commodities like crude oil and copper have also fallen (Chart 1.1), indicating that global
growth prospects have dimmed. As a measure of the heightened degree of risk aversion in
markets, the VIX rose sharply to a level that had previously only been breached during the
COVID-19 pandemic and the Global Financial Crisis (Chart 1.2).

Global monetary policy has remained on a broad easing path. While the US Federal Reserve
paused its series of rate cuts in December 2024, US 2-year yields have moderated since
January (Chart 1.4). Meanwhile, the European Central Bank has lowered its deposit facility

pean Central Bank has lowered its deposit facility rate to 2.25%, while the Bank of Japan paused its rate hike cycle. In the region, the 7-day
reverse repo rate in China has remained stable since the beginning of this year.

2 The International Economy
2.1 Recent Developments
Global growth momentum expanded at a firm pace in 2024
The global economy expanded at a steady pace in Q4 last year, bringing overall growth for
2024 to a creditable 3.2%1 (Chart 2.1). The two key growth drivers for the year—robust
domestic demand in the US and strong export performance in Asia—had underpinned the Q4
outturn (Chart 2.2). The US economy saw strong consumer spending alongside continued

y saw strong consumer spending alongside continued investment in ICT equipment and software. Meanwhile, the strength of US final demand
combined with frontloading by US importers in anticipation of tariffs, had lent support to the
production and export activities of China and ASEAN.

2.2 Outlook
The sharp escalation in trade frictions has negatively impacted prospects for
global trade and growth
The escalation of trade frictions, involving the increase of import tariffs by the US and
retaliatory measures by some of its trade partners, will have serious ramifications for the
global economy. The US is deeply plugged into global trade, accounting for almost a quarter
of flows in the world’s trade corridors (Chart 2.5).

flows in the world’s trade corridors (Chart 2.5). Economies that levy duties on imports will likely experience an increase in costs, which will
in turn weigh on business and consumer spending. Meanwhile, exporting countries which
have been hit by the US tariffs will experience a negative externally induced shock on export
production. Beyond the impact from trade channels, heightened policy uncertainty and the
broader tightening in financial conditions will cause businesses and households to defer

ions will cause businesses and households to defer investment and discretionary spending, exerting further downward pressure on growth.
The uncertainty surrounding the global growth outlook remains elevated. Global growth could
slip further if the US broadens and intensifies the tariffs to include other specific products
such as semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. Retaliatory actions by its trading partners will
exacerbate the impact on growth. Market sentiments also remain fragile, and further shocks

sentiments also remain fragile, and further shocks could trigger disorderly price adjustments, with the ensuing tightening in financial conditions
compounding the downturn.
Global GDP growth is expected to slow significantly to between 2.0–2.5% in 2025, from 3.2%
last year. There is significant uncertainty around US trade policy and its impact.
Price pressures should remain contained notwithstanding tariff-induced price
increases in the US
Global headline inflation is projected to average 2.3% in 2025, slightly higher than the 2.1%

verage 2.3% in 2025, slightly higher than the 2.1% seen last year. In the US, the tariffs will impart a fresh and significant shock to prices, even as
its growth moderates and wage growth eases amid elevated concerns about the negative
demand impact.
However, the impact on price pressures should remain contained as the rest of the world
faces downward price pressures amid the tariff-induced negative demand shock. Commodity
prices are expected to ease due to softer global demand and positive supply-side factors,

er global demand and positive supply-side factors, such as the planned oil supply increases by OPEC countries for crude oil and favourable
weather conditions for food production.
Within the region, China’s disinflationary pressures could persist. This reflects subdued
consumer confidence and significant economic slack. Domestic price pressures in ASEAN
will also remain modest in view of below-trend growth.

3 The Singapore Economy
3.1 Recent Developments
Growth in the Singapore economy eased in Q1 2025
Economic activity in Singapore showed signs of softening at the start of this year. Based on
the Advance Estimates, Singapore’s GDP contracted by 0.8% q-o-q SA in Q1 2025, a pullback
from the 0.5% expansion in Q4 2024 (Chart 3.1). On a y-o-y basis, GDP growth moderated to
3.8% from 5.0% in the previous quarter. The weaker performance was driven by the trade-

r. The weaker performance was driven by the trade- related and modern services sectors, while activity in the domestic-facing industries such as
construction and retail were generally tepid1 (Chart 3.2). While there are nascent indications
of economic softening at the sectoral level, these weaknesses have yet to become deeply
entrenched or pervasive.

3.2 Outlook
The external global economic landscape has become more uncertain
Singapore’s growth outlook has turned more cautious amid an increasingly challenging global
trade environment. On 2 April, the US imposed reciprocal tariffs of at least 10% on nearly all

d reciprocal tariffs of at least 10% on nearly all countries, with exemptions on certain products. Singapore was subjected to the 10% baseline
tariff rate, while various other countries were hit by higher tariffs. Shortly after on 9 April, a 90-
day pause was announced on the higher-band tariffs except those imposed on China, but the
baseline 10% tariff and the 25% tariff on all US imports of steel, aluminium, and automobiles
& parts implemented earlier remain in place.

biles
& parts implemented earlier remain in place. The impact of the tariffs will propagate through multiplier effects and generate a broader
negative income and demand shock to the Singapore economy. GDP growth will also be
impacted through the confidence channel by the continuing uncertainty surrounding the
eventual effective tariff, which will induce a broader retraction in global consumption and
investment spending. (See Box A on an empirical analysis of the impact of policy uncertainty

rical analysis of the impact of policy uncertainty on the Singapore economy). The transmissions to production, income, consumption and
ultimately overall GDP growth for Singapore will occur through three main stages:
• The increase in the global tariff rate is akin to a production tax on Singapore’s
producers and exporters who are price takers in world markets. With Singapore’s
terms of trade (excluding oil) already weakening since mid-2024, additional tariffs
would only exacerbate the strain by squeezing export margins.

exacerbate the strain by squeezing export margins. • Singapore firms then reduce their production levels as the short-term profit
maximising level of output falls. Consequently, domestic producers are hit by falling
corporate incomes/profits, which could lead to less demand for factors of
production.

Besides the direct impact from tariffs, Singapore will be affected indirectly via
the impact of tariffs on the region
Reciprocal tariffs account for the bulk of the direct tariff impact on Singapore. The US is

f the direct tariff impact on Singapore. The US is Singapore’s second largest market, accounting for 11% of Singapore’s domestic exports in
2024. Products subject to the baseline tariff accounted for about 55% of Singapore’s
domestic exports to the US, with product-specific tariffs on steel, aluminium, and automobiles
& parts comprising a much smaller share of around 5% (Table 3.1). Although exempted
products such as semiconductors, consumer electronics and pharmaceutical goods, which

nsumer electronics and pharmaceutical goods, which account for about 40% of Singapore’s domestic exports to the US, are currently not subject to
tariffs, the US administration has initiated trade probes into imports of these goods on
national security concerns and could impose restrictions in the coming months.

Box A: The Impact of Policy Uncertainty on the Singapore Economy
The Global Economic Policy Uncertainty (GEPU) index (Baker, Bloom, and Davis, 2016)
reached an all-time high in early 2025 (Chart A1), predominantly reflecting a surge in
uncertainty over US trade policy. Apart from the trade-dampening effects of the implemented
tariffs itself, uncertainty around the scale, scope and permanence of trade barriers could also

scope and permanence of trade barriers could also impact business and consumer spending. Firms are likely to hold back on their investments
due to the increased level of uncertainty (Dixit and Pindyck, 1994), while borrowing costs
could also rise with greater risk premia. Meanwhile, households may reduce spending to build
up precautionary buffers against potential income shocks. This Box explores the quantitative
effects of global policy uncertainty on the Singapore economy.

following SVAR model is estimated from Q2 1998 to Q4 2024:

Impulse Response Functions
The impulse response functions (IRFs) from the SVAR model indicate that global policy
uncertainty has statistically significant adverse impacts on the Singapore economy. In
response to a positive one standard deviation (SD) shock to the GEPU index (or a 20.4%

deviation (SD) shock to the GEPU index (or a 20.4% increase in uncertainty), private non-residential investment registers a cumulative 3.6%
decline after four quarters while private consumption falls 1.5% within the same period (Chart
A2). Overall, real GDP is 1.3% below baseline a year after the shock—similar to the 1.0%
estimated by Luk et al. (2017) for Hong Kong but larger than the 0.5% estimated by the
European Commission (2024) for the Eurozone.2
Chart A2 Cumulative Responses to A Positive One SD GEPU Shock from the SVAR Model

o A Positive One SD GEPU Shock from the SVAR Model Source: EPG, MAS estimates
Note: Shaded regions represent the 95% confidence interval.
The increase in the EBP in Chart A2 indicates that global credit conditions tighten in
response to higher global policy uncertainty, in line with findings from other studies (e.g.,
Caldara et al., 2016). This affects Singapore’s GDP and points to the presence of a financial
channel through which global policy uncertainty affects real economic activity.

policy uncertainty affects real economic activity. As a robustness check, the IRFs from the SVAR are compared with those from a local
projection model (Jordà, 2005) where the dependent real variables are, separately,
Singapore’s private consumption, private non-residential investment and GDP. The IRFs are
similar; in both models, investment is impacted more greatly than consumption, while the
impact on Singapore’s GDP is greatest four quarters after the shock. Notably, leveraging on

r quarters after the shock. Notably, leveraging on the flexibility enabled by the local projection method, there is evidence of an asymmetry in the

Sum-Up
The analysis in this Box suggests that the sharp rise in global policy uncertainty since

the sharp rise in global policy uncertainty since late last year will weigh on Singapore’s growth. Private non-residential investment is likely to
pull back by more than consumption. At the same time, the tightening of global financial
conditions could reinforce the drag on real economic activity in Singapore.

Box B: Macroeconomic Assessment of Budget 2025
This Box item provides an overview and assessment of the key economic initiatives in
Budget 2025.
Macroeconomic Backdrop
Budget 2025 was delivered in February in the context of relative macroeconomic balance,
compared to the previous years of high inflation and volatile growth. At the end of 2024, GDP
was estimated to be slightly above the economy’s potential, while labour market conditions

conomy’s potential, while labour market conditions had largely normalised following a period of tightness. In addition, inflation had fallen
significantly with MAS Core Inflation coming in below 2% y-o-y after an extended period of
elevated inflation.
Nonetheless, elevated costs and prices had remained a concern. In particular, while real
wages in Singapore grew faster over 2020–24 than in many regional economies as well as
some advanced economies, the pace of real wage gains has slowed slightly compared to the

eal wage gains has slowed slightly compared to the pre-pandemic period (2010–19). Further, heightened geopolitical and trade policy uncertainty
posed additional downside risks to Singapore’s small and trade-dependent economy, and
consequently jobs and livelihoods. Indeed, Singapore’s GDP growth is expected to slow in
2025 amid tariff-related weakening in external demand and heightened uncertainty. The
balance of risks was therefore tilted towards growth weakening by more than expected, and
in turn inflation falling more discernibly.

d, and
in turn inflation falling more discernibly. Near-term Support Measures to Alleviate Cost Pressures
Against this backdrop, Budget 2025 sought to address near-term cyclical concerns as
well as longer-term priorities.
The Budget provided further support for coping with cost-of-living pressures, especially
to lower- and middle-income households. For instance, there were additional Community
Development Council (CDC) Vouchers for all Singaporean households, U-Save rebates to help

all Singaporean households, U-Save rebates to help with utilities expenses for eligible HDB households, as well as enhanced support for lower-
income households under the ComCare Assistance schemes.
This year’s Budget also included an SG60 package to recognise the contributions of all
Singaporeans, and to share the benefits of the nation’s progress. The package included SG60
Vouchers for all Singaporeans aged 21 and above this year, as well as a Personal Income Tax

above this year, as well as a Personal Income Tax Rebate of 60% for the Year of Assessment 2025, capped at $200, to mostly benefit middle-
income workers.
In a similar vein to previous Budgets, Budget 2025 also continued to provide support for
firms grappling with higher costs. The Progressive Wage Credit Scheme was enhanced, by
increasing the Government’s co-funding levels for wage increases. A 50% Corporate Income
Tax Rebate was also introduced for the Year of Assessment 2025, capped at $40,000, with

e Year of Assessment 2025, capped at $40,000, with every active company that employed at least one local employee last year receiving a
minimum benefit of $2,000.

e last year receiving a
minimum benefit of $2,000. The Singapore Economy 29
Structural Measures to Sustain Growth and Inclusivity
Aside from addressing near-term concerns, the Budget continued to build on ongoing
efforts to strengthen the supply-side of the economy by enhancing labour quality and building
up the capital stock.
To boost the potential of the resident workforce, the Budget enhanced the Workfare
Skills Support (WSS) scheme, to provide lower-wage workers aged 30 years and above with

de lower-wage workers aged 30 years and above with monthly training allowances when they enrol in selected long-form part-time or full-time
courses. This policy will help to alleviate the sharp income trade-off that lower-wage workers
face when they forgo work to upskill themselves.
To bolster the capital stock, a significant sum ($19.6 billion, 2.6% of GDP) was set aside
to top up funds that will finance spending into the medium term on critical areas such as

ing into the medium term on critical areas such as preparing the nation for climate change, exploring clean energies, investing in productivity
and R&D initiatives, as well as the construction of Changi Airport Terminal 5. The use of fund
top-ups secures the Government’s ability to continue with investments in the Singapore
economy, to support long-term needs in a sustainable way, regardless of shifting cyclical
conditions.
Budget 2025 also enhanced efforts to reduce inequality, support families and improve

to reduce inequality, support families and improve social mobility—important priorities for Singaporeans, as revealed by the Forward Singapore
exercise. The Budget enhanced the Fresh Start Housing Scheme, which helps families with
children living in public rental flats to own their own homes by buying a shorter-lease new flat
with a housing grant. There were also measures to reduce preschool expenses, provide more
support to large families with three or more children, and improve the affordability of long-

e children, and improve the affordability of long- term care expenses. Labour policies that incentivise employers to hire more vulnerable
groups who might experience greater difficulties finding employment, such as seniors, ex-
offenders, and persons with disabilities, were also extended.
Quantitative Assessment of Budget 2025
On a calendar year basis, the fiscal impulse (FI), which gauges the net fiscal support to
GDP from the change in the cyclically adjusted budget balance, is estimated to be a modest

justed budget balance, is estimated to be a modest +0.4% of GDP.1 This is a mildly supportive impulse for the economy in 2025.
To quantify the impact of Budget 2025 in a general equilibrium context and account for
its dynamic effects, key measures were simulated using the Monetary Model of Singapore
(MMS), MAS’ flagship macroeconomic model.
Budget 2025 is estimated to lift real disposable incomes by about 1% in 2025 through
the SG60 package and cost relief measures. This will boost the level of real GDP by about 0.4%

his will boost the level of real GDP by about 0.4% (Chart B1), mainly through stronger consumption growth compared to a counterfactual
scenario without these discretionary fiscal injections.

Expenditure, which is computed for FY2025. On a FY basis, the fiscal impulse is projected to be +0.9% of GDP
in FY2025. The difference reflects lumpiness in the quarterly profile of projected revenues and spending.
rterly profile of projected revenues and spending. Adjusting for this difference in timing, the FI estimate over a four-quarter period would be similar.

The impact of Budget 2025 on inflation is expected to be negligible this year and only
modestly positive in 2026 as the delayed transmission from the boost to aggregate demand
this year raises prices incrementally.2 The muted response of inflation to the fiscal stimulus

muted response of inflation to the fiscal stimulus in 2025 also reflects the fact that the bulk of the disbursements are scheduled to be paid out
only in the latter half of this year.
MAS’ simulations also suggest that Budget 2025 will continue to provide some support
to the real economy in the medium term because of measures that seek to lift capital
accumulation and the quality of the workforce. These injections will enable improvements in
aggregate supply by raising firm and worker productivity and in turn, bolster the economy’s

er productivity and in turn, bolster the economy’s productive potential.
All in, Budget 2025 is assessed to maintain an appropriately expansionary impulse amid
the challenging external environment, while supporting longer-term efforts to enhance the
economy’s resilience and competitiveness.

4.1 Labour Market1
Employment growth slowed amid weaker hiring in the services sectors, while
labour cost increases remained below their recent peaks
In Q4 2024, total employment growth excluding migrant domestic workers (MDWs) stepped
down from the previous quarter. While employment growth in the trade-related sector picked
up alongside the manufacturing upturn in H2 last year, hiring in both the domestic-oriented

H2 last year, hiring in both the domestic-oriented and outward-facing modern services clusters eased (Chart 4.1). In particular, employment
growth in the domestic-oriented services sector slowed to its weakest pace since Q1 2022,
despite the year-end festivities.

in external growth prospects and subdued consumer spending locally. Persistent softness in
domestic retail and F&B sales led to tepid hiring in these sectors. In addition, headcount in
the Information & Communications (I&C) sector contracted in Q4 on the back of weaker
operating revenue growth, likely as firms in the wider economy saw some consolidation in

rms in the wider economy saw some consolidation in technology investments following robust growth earlier in the year (Chart 4.2).
Early signs of softening in the labour market emerged in the last quarter of 2024, after several
years of tightness. The resident unemployment rate, retrenchment rate as well as the number
of workers placed on short work-week or temporary layoff all ticked up slightly in Q4 2024
from the previous quarter, while the long-term unemployment rate stayed above its historical

term unemployment rate stayed above its historical average. Nevertheless, most indicators remained close to or below their pre-COVID norms,
indicating that as of Q4 2024, the labour market remained in a state of broad demand-supply
balance (Chart 4.3).

Labour demand could soften further this year, accompanied by slower wage
growth
Aggregate demand for workers is likely to moderate this year in tandem with the cyclical
weakening in the economy. As firms pull back on their hiring and expansion plans amid

pull back on their hiring and expansion plans amid economic uncertainty, employment growth for most sectors should step down from last year.
In particular, the trade-related sectors of manufacturing and wholesale trade could see
greater employment weakness given the increasingly challenging global trade environment.

non-resident workers. These workers account for a large share of employment in the trade-

ount for a large share of employment in the trade- related sectors. Demand for resident workers in manpower-constrained sectors such as
healthcare should remain resilient. According to the latest ManpowerGroup Employment

4.2 Inflation Developments
Inflation slowed further and came in below expectations in Q1
MAS Core Inflation fell significantly to 0.6% y-o-y in Q1 from 1.9% in Q4 2024, while CPI-All
Items inflation moderated to 1.0% from 1.4% (Chart 4.7). In both instances, the pace of price
increases was weaker than expected. The decline in core inflation in Q1 compared to the
previous quarter was driven to a large extent by a broad-based weakening in underlying price

ent by a broad-based weakening in underlying price pressures.
The low inflation prints reflect a strengthening of the disinflation trend that began in mid-
2024. By Q4 last year, inflation momentum had fallen to low levels. It has since weakened
further, turning negative in Q1 (Chart 4.8). Alternative measures of underlying inflation that
abstract from more volatile price components also indicated continued disinflation.

correspondingly, in inflation pressures (Table 4.1). By contrast, the low inflation in 2015 was

e 4.1). By contrast, the low inflation in 2015 was more narrowly based, primarily driven by a large fall in energy- and travel-related prices due to

Continuing lacklustre consumer spending locally was likely a factor dampening inflation.
Retail and F&B sales volumes have been tepid for several quarters, with the latter remaining
well below pre-COVID levels (Chart 4.9). Pressures on real wage growth as well as some
expenditure switching abroad due to the elevated S$ real effective exchange rate (S$REER)
have likely been factors dampening consumption expenditure in Singapore. Moreover,

ng consumption expenditure in Singapore. Moreover, competitive pressures in sectors such as F&B may have increased, reducing the ability of
firms to pass through cost increases.3
Chart 4.9: Retail and F&B Sales Volumes Chart 4.10: Import Price Indices
Source: DOS Source: DOS
Meanwhile, business cost pressures have also remained relatively modest. In recent months,
Singapore’s oil and non-oil import prices have continued to fall on a y-o-y basis, lowering the

e continued to fall on a y-o-y basis, lowering the cost of utilities and imported intermediate goods (Chart 4.10). While the cost of food imports
rose, this was driven mainly by sharp increases in a narrow group of items such as coffee and
cocoa, the prices of which have risen by over 30% y-o-y in recent months amid poor harvests
globally. Excluding these items, food import prices have risen more modestly by 2.8% y-o-y in
Jan–Feb. On the domestic front, commercial rents for retail spaces have also stayed broadly

l rents for retail spaces have also stayed broadly flat since 2021, while services ULC grew at a moderate pace of 1.4% y-o-y in 2024, in line with
its historical average.
Against this backdrop, inflation fell across a broad range of CPI categories in Q1. Retail &
other goods prices continued to register outright declines from year-ago levels while
discretionary services inflation eased. Notably, inflation for food services and essential
services both fell below their historical norms with the latter partly reflecting enhanced

l norms with the latter partly reflecting enhanced government subsidies. Overall, inflation rates for most broad components of the CPI basket
are now below their historical averages (Chart 4.11).

Both MAS Core and CPI-All Items inflation are now projected to average 0.5–1.5% in 2025.
The downgrade in the core inflation forecast, from 1.0–2.0% previously, reflects the weaker-
than-expected inflation outturns in Q1 as well as expectations that domestic demand and

1 as well as expectations that domestic demand and business cost pressures will come in more moderate than previously anticipated (Chart 4.12).
The CPI-All Items inflation forecast range has also been lowered, from 1.5–2.5% previously,
due to both the step-down in the core inflation forecast and softer-than-anticipated headline
inflation at the beginning of the year.
